title: Android (operating system)

infobox.value: [Open source](/wiki/Open_source_software "Open source software"), [freeware](/wiki/Freeware "Freeware") (most devices include [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") components, such as [Google Mobile Services](/wiki/Google_Mobile_Services "Google Mobile Services"))

[Smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), [smart TVs](/wiki/Smart_TV "Smart TV") ([Android TV](/wiki/Android_TV "Android TV")), cars ([Android Automotive](/wiki/Android_Automotive "Android Automotive")) and [smartwatches](/wiki/Smartwatch "Smartwatch") ([Wear OS](/wiki/Wear_OS "Wear OS"))

[64-bit](/wiki/64-bit_computing "64-bit computing"): [ARM64](/wiki/AArch64 "AArch64"), [x86-64](/wiki/X86-64 "X86-64") [32-bit](/wiki/32-bit_computing "32-bit computing") (still supported, mostly old devices; apps need to support 64-bit too): [ARMv7](/wiki/ARM_architecture_family#32-bit_architecture "ARM architecture family"), [x86](/wiki/X86 "X86")
* [Apache License](/wiki/Apache_License "Apache License") 2.0 for userspace software * [GNU GPL](/wiki/GNU_GPL "GNU GPL") v2 for the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel") modifications

is an [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") owned by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") which is based on a modified version of the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel") and other [open-source software](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software"), designed primarily for [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen")-based mobile devices such as [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"). Android has historically been developed by a [consortium](/wiki/Consortium "Consortium") of [developers](/wiki/Software_development "Software development") known as the [Open Handset Alliance](/wiki/Open_Handset_Alliance "Open Handset Alliance"), but its most widely used version is primarily developed by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"). First released in 2008, Android is the world's [most widely used operating system](/wiki/Usage_share_of_operating_systems "Usage share of operating systems"); it is the most used operating system for smartphones, and [also most used for tablets](#Platform_information);[[2]](#cite_note-tablet_stats-2) the latest version, released on June 10, 2025, is [Android 16](/wiki/Android_16 "Android 16").

(**AOSP**)[[3]](#cite_note-3) and is [free and open-source software](/wiki/Free_and_open-source_software "Free and open-source software") (FOSS) primarily licensed under the [Apache License](/wiki/Apache_License "Apache License"). However, most devices run the [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") Android version developed by Google, which ships with additional proprietary closed-source software pre-installed,[[4]](#cite_note-4) most notably [Google Mobile Services](/wiki/Google_Mobile_Services "Google Mobile Services") (GMS),[[5]](#cite_note-5) which includes core apps such as [Google Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome"), the [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") platform [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play"), and the associated [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services") development platform. Other Google services including [Firebase Cloud Messaging](/wiki/Firebase_Cloud_Messaging "Firebase Cloud Messaging"), used for push notifications, are recommended for applications. While AOSP is free, the "Android" name and logo are [trademarks](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") of Google, who restrict the use of Android branding on "uncertified" products.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The majority of smartphones based on AOSP run Google's ecosystem—which is known simply as Android—some with [vendor](/wiki/Vendor "Vendor")-customized user interfaces and software suites,[[8]](#cite_note-8) for example [One UI](/wiki/One_UI "One UI"). Numerous [modified distributions](/wiki/List_of_custom_Android_distributions "List of custom Android distributions") exist, which include competing [Amazon Fire OS](/wiki/Fire_OS "Fire OS"), community-developed [LineageOS](/wiki/LineageOS "LineageOS"); the source code has also been used to develop a variety of Android distributions on a range of other devices, such as [Android TV](/wiki/Android_TV "Android TV") for televisions, [Wear OS](/wiki/Wear_OS "Wear OS") for [wearables](/wiki/Wearable_computer "Wearable computer"), and [Android Automotive](/wiki/Android_Automotive "Android Automotive") for in-car systems. Commercial products like [micro consoles](/wiki/Micro_consoles "Micro consoles") and [virtual reality headset](/wiki/Virtual_reality_headset "Virtual reality headset") have also used Android.

was founded in [Palo Alto, California](/wiki/Palo_Alto,_California "Palo Alto, California"), in October 2003 by [Andy Rubin](/wiki/Andy_Rubin "Andy Rubin") and Chris White, with [Rich Miner](/wiki/Rich_Miner "Rich Miner") and Nick Sears[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Google_Buys_Android-14) joining later. Rubin had previously been the creator of the [T-Mobile Sidekick](/wiki/T-Mobile_Sidekick "T-Mobile Sidekick") under his previous company [Danger, Inc.](/wiki/Danger,_Inc. "Danger, Inc.")[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) Rubin and White started out to build an operating system for [digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera") viz *FotoFrame*. The company name was changed to *Android* as Rubin already owned the [domain name](/wiki/Domain_name "Domain name") `android.com`. After having built a prototype internally known as the "Fadden demo" predominantly by purchasing licensing agreements for most of the software components built around a custom [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript") front-end, the company failed to convince investors, and so in April 2004 they pivoted to building an operating system for phones at the suggestion of Nick Sears,[[15]](#cite_note-hasse1-17)[[16]](#cite_note-pcworld-camera-os-18) as a rival to [Symbian](/wiki/Symbian "Symbian") and Microsoft [Windows Mobile](/wiki/Windows_Mobile "Windows Mobile").[[17]](#cite_note-19) Rubin pitched the Android project as having "tremendous potential in developing smarter mobile devices that are more aware of its owner's location and preferences".[[12]](#cite_note-Google_Buys_Android-14) Due to difficulty attracting investors early on, Android faced potential eviction from its office space. [Steve Perlman](/wiki/Steve_Perlman_(entrepreneur) "Steve Perlman (entrepreneur)"), a close friend of Rubin, brought him $10,000 in cash in an envelope, and shortly thereafter wired an undisclosed amount as seed funding. Perlman refused a stake in the company, and has stated "I did it because I believed in the thing, and I wanted to help Andy."[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)

Operating system for mobile devices

Linux distribution

| Android | |
| --- | --- |
| [A flat robot head, a green semicircle with antennas and small holes for eyes](/wiki/File:Android_2023_3D_logo_and_wordmark.svg) Wordmark and logo used since 2023 | |
| [Developer](/wiki/Programmer "Programmer") | [Open Handset Alliance](/wiki/Open_Handset_Alliance "Open Handset Alliance") (led by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google")) |
| [Written in](/wiki/Programming_language "Programming language") | [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)"), [Kotlin](/wiki/Kotlin "Kotlin") (UI), [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)") (core), [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++"), [Rust](/wiki/Rust_(programming_language) "Rust (programming language)")[[1]](#cite_note-1) and others |
| OS family | [Unix-like](/wiki/Unix-like "Unix-like") (modified [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel")) |
| Working state | Active |
| Source model | [Open source](/wiki/Open_source_software "Open source software"), [freeware](/wiki/Freeware "Freeware") (most devices include [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") components, such as [Google Mobile Services](/wiki/Google_Mobile_Services "Google Mobile Services")) |
| Initial release | [Android 1.0](/wiki/Android_1.0 "Android 1.0") / September 23, 2008; 17 years ago (2008-09-23) |
| [Latest release](/wiki/Software_release_life_cycle "Software release life cycle") | [Android 16](/wiki/Android_16 "Android 16") / June 10, 2025; 9 months ago (2025-06-10) |
| [Repository](/wiki/Repository_(version_control) "Repository (version control)") | * [android.googlesource.com](https://android.googlesource.com/) [Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q94#P1324 "Edit this at Wikidata") |
| Marketing target | [Smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), [smart TVs](/wiki/Smart_TV "Smart TV") ([Android TV](/wiki/Android_TV "Android TV")), cars ([Android Automotive](/wiki/Android_Automotive "Android Automotive")) and [smartwatches](/wiki/Smartwatch "Smartwatch") ([Wear OS](/wiki/Wear_OS "Wear OS")) |
| Available in | 100+ languages |
| Update method | [Over-the-air](/wiki/Over-the-air_update "Over-the-air update") |

| [Default user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") | [Graphical](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface") ([multi-touch](/wiki/Multi-touch "Multi-touch")) |

**Android** is an [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") owned by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") which is based on a modified version of the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel") and other [open-source software](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software"), designed primarily for [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen")-based mobile devices such as [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"). Android has historically been developed by a [consortium](/wiki/Consortium "Consortium") of [developers](/wiki/Software_development "Software development") known as the [Open Handset Alliance](/wiki/Open_Handset_Alliance "Open Handset Alliance"), but its most widely used version is primarily developed by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"). First released in 2008, Android is the world's [most widely used operating system](/wiki/Usage_share_of_operating_systems "Usage share of operating systems"); it is the most used operating system for smartphones, and [also most used for tablets](#Platform_information);[[2]](#cite_note-tablet_stats-2) the latest version, released on June 10, 2025, is [Android 16](/wiki/Android_16 "Android 16").

At its core, the operating system is known as the **Android Open Source Project** (**AOSP**)[[3]](#cite_note-3) and is [free and open-source software](/wiki/Free_and_open-source_software "Free and open-source software") (FOSS) primarily licensed under the [Apache License](/wiki/Apache_License "Apache License"). However, most devices run the [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") Android version developed by Google, which ships with additional proprietary closed-source software pre-installed,[[4]](#cite_note-4) most notably [Google Mobile Services](/wiki/Google_Mobile_Services "Google Mobile Services") (GMS),[[5]](#cite_note-5) which includes core apps such as [Google Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome"), the [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") platform [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play"), and the associated [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services") development platform. Other Google services including [Firebase Cloud Messaging](/wiki/Firebase_Cloud_Messaging "Firebase Cloud Messaging"), used for push notifications, are recommended for applications. While AOSP is free, the "Android" name and logo are [trademarks](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") of Google, who restrict the use of Android branding on "uncertified" products.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The majority of smartphones based on AOSP run Google's ecosystem—which is known simply as Android—some with [vendor](/wiki/Vendor "Vendor")-customized user interfaces and software suites,[[8]](#cite_note-8) for example [One UI](/wiki/One_UI "One UI"). Numerous [modified distributions](/wiki/List_of_custom_Android_distributions "List of custom Android distributions") exist, which include competing [Amazon Fire OS](/wiki/Fire_OS "Fire OS"), community-developed [LineageOS](/wiki/LineageOS "LineageOS"); the source code has also been used to develop a variety of Android distributions on a range of other devices, such as [Android TV](/wiki/Android_TV "Android TV") for televisions, [Wear OS](/wiki/Wear_OS "Wear OS") for [wearables](/wiki/Wearable_computer "Wearable computer"), and [Android Automotive](/wiki/Android_Automotive "Android Automotive") for in-car systems. Commercial products like [micro consoles](/wiki/Micro_consoles "Micro consoles") and [virtual reality headset](/wiki/Virtual_reality_headset "Virtual reality headset") have also used Android.

Software packages on Android, which use the [APK](/wiki/Apk_(file_format) "Apk (file format)") format, are generally distributed through a proprietary [application store](/wiki/Application_store "Application store"); non-Google platforms include vendor-specific [Amazon Appstore](/wiki/Amazon_Appstore "Amazon Appstore"), [Samsung Galaxy Store](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Store "Samsung Galaxy Store"), [Huawei AppGallery](/wiki/Huawei_AppGallery "Huawei AppGallery"), and third-party companies [Aptoide](/wiki/Aptoide "Aptoide"), [Cafe Bazaar](/wiki/Cafe_Bazaar "Cafe Bazaar"), [GetJar](/wiki/GetJar "GetJar") or open source [F-Droid](/wiki/F-Droid "F-Droid"). Since 2011 Android has been the most used operating system worldwide on smartphones. It has the largest [installed base](/wiki/Installed_base "Installed base") of any operating system in the world[[9]](#cite_note-9) with over three billion [monthly active users](/wiki/Monthly_active_users "Monthly active users")[[a]](#cite_note-10) and accounting for 46% of the global operating system market.[[b]](#cite_note-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12)

## History

See also: [Android version history](/wiki/Android_version_history "Android version history")

### 2000s

**Android Inc.** was founded in [Palo Alto, California](/wiki/Palo_Alto,_California "Palo Alto, California"), in October 2003 by [Andy Rubin](/wiki/Andy_Rubin "Andy Rubin") and Chris White, with [Rich Miner](/wiki/Rich_Miner "Rich Miner") and Nick Sears[[11]](#cite_note-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Google_Buys_Android-14) joining later. Rubin had previously been the creator of the [T-Mobile Sidekick](/wiki/T-Mobile_Sidekick "T-Mobile Sidekick") under his previous company [Danger, Inc.](/wiki/Danger,_Inc. "Danger, Inc.")[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) Rubin and White started out to build an operating system for [digital cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera") viz *FotoFrame*. The company name was changed to *Android* as Rubin already owned the [domain name](/wiki/Domain_name "Domain name") `android.com`. After having built a prototype internally known as the "Fadden demo" predominantly by purchasing licensing agreements for most of the software components built around a custom [JavaScript](/wiki/JavaScript "JavaScript") front-end, the company failed to convince investors, and so in April 2004 they pivoted to building an operating system for phones at the suggestion of Nick Sears,[[15]](#cite_note-hasse1-17)[[16]](#cite_note-pcworld-camera-os-18) as a rival to [Symbian](/wiki/Symbian "Symbian") and Microsoft [Windows Mobile](/wiki/Windows_Mobile "Windows Mobile").[[17]](#cite_note-19) Rubin pitched the Android project as having "tremendous potential in developing smarter mobile devices that are more aware of its owner's location and preferences".[[12]](#cite_note-Google_Buys_Android-14) Due to difficulty attracting investors early on, Android faced potential eviction from its office space. [Steve Perlman](/wiki/Steve_Perlman_(entrepreneur) "Steve Perlman (entrepreneur)"), a close friend of Rubin, brought him $10,000 in cash in an envelope, and shortly thereafter wired an undisclosed amount as seed funding. Perlman refused a stake in the company, and has stated "I did it because I believed in the thing, and I wanted to help Andy."[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)

In 2005, Rubin tried to negotiate deals with [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung")[[20]](#cite_note-MPC-22) and [HTC](/wiki/HTC "HTC").[[21]](#cite_note-23) Shortly afterwards, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") acquired the company in July of that year for at least $50 million;[[12]](#cite_note-Google_Buys_Android-14)[[22]](#cite_note-Murky_road_despite_dominance-24) this was Google's "best deal ever" according to Google's then-vice president of corporate development, [David Lawee](/wiki/David_Lawee "David Lawee"), in 2010.[[20]](#cite_note-MPC-22) Android's key employees, including Rubin, Miner, Sears, and White, joined Google as part of the acquisition.[[12]](#cite_note-Google_Buys_Android-14) Not much was known about the secretive Android Inc. at the time, with the company having provided few details other than that it was making software for mobile phones.[[12]](#cite_note-Google_Buys_Android-14) At Google, the team led by Rubin developed a mobile device platform powered by the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel"). Google marketed the platform to [handset makers](/wiki/Original_equipment_manufacturer "Original equipment manufacturer") and [carriers](/wiki/Mobile_network_operator "Mobile network operator") on the promise of providing a flexible, upgradeable system.[[23]](#cite_note-25) Google had "lined up a series of hardware components and software partners and signaled to carriers that it was open to various degrees of cooperation".[*[attribution needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Attribution_needed "Wikipedia:Attribution needed")*][[24]](#cite_note-26)

Speculation about Google's intention to enter the mobile communications market continued to build through December 2006.[[25]](#cite_note-27) An early [prototype](/wiki/Prototype "Prototype") had a close resemblance to a [BlackBerry](/wiki/BlackBerry "BlackBerry") phone, with no touchscreen and a physical [QWERTY](/wiki/QWERTY "QWERTY") [keyboard](/wiki/Computer_keyboard "Computer keyboard"), but the arrival of [Apple's](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") 2007 [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") meant that Android "had to go back to the drawing board".[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29) Google later changed its Android specification documents to state that "Touchscreens will be supported", although "the Product was designed with the presence of discrete physical buttons as an assumption, therefore a touchscreen cannot completely replace physical buttons".[[28]](#cite_note-30) By 2008, both [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") and BlackBerry announced touch-based smartphones to rival the [iPhone 3G](/wiki/IPhone_3G "IPhone 3G"), and Android's focus eventually switched to just touchscreens. The first commercially available smartphone running Android was the [HTC Dream](/wiki/HTC_Dream "HTC Dream"), also known as T-Mobile G1, announced on September 23, 2008.[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/HTC_Android_T-Mobile_G1.jpg/250px-HTC_Android_T-Mobile_G1.jpg)](/wiki/File:HTC_Android_T-Mobile_G1.jpg)

[HTC Dream](/wiki/HTC_Dream "HTC Dream") or T-Mobile G1, the first commercially released device running Android (2008)

On November 5, 2007, the [Open Handset Alliance](/wiki/Open_Handset_Alliance "Open Handset Alliance"), a [consortium](/wiki/Consortium "Consortium") of technology companies including Google, device manufacturers such as HTC, [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola_Mobility "Motorola Mobility") and Samsung, wireless carriers such as [Sprint](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation") and [T-Mobile](/wiki/T-Mobile_US "T-Mobile US"), and chipset makers such as [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm") and [Texas Instruments](/wiki/Texas_Instruments "Texas Instruments"), unveiled itself, with a goal to develop "the first truly open and comprehensive platform for mobile devices".[[31]](#cite_note-Announcement_of_OHA-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35) Within a year, the Open Handset Alliance faced two other [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") competitors, the [Symbian Foundation](/wiki/Symbian_Foundation "Symbian Foundation") and the [LiMo Foundation](/wiki/LiMo_Foundation "LiMo Foundation"), the latter also developing a [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux")-based mobile operating system like Google. In September 2007, Google had filed several [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent") applications in the area of mobile telephony.[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-37)[[36]](#cite_note-38)

On September 23, 2008, Android was introduced by Andy Rubin, Larry Page, Sergey Brin, Cole Brodman, Christopher Schlaeffer and Peter Chou at a press conference in a [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") [subway station](/wiki/Subway_station "Subway station").[[37]](#cite_note-39)

Since 2008, Android has seen [numerous updates](/wiki/Android_version_history "Android version history") which have incrementally improved the operating system, adding new features and fixing [bugs](/wiki/Software_bug "Software bug") in previous releases. The first two Android versions were internally codenamed [Astro Boy](/wiki/Astro_Boy "Astro Boy") and [Bender](/wiki/Bender_(Futurama) "Bender (Futurama)") but licensing issues meant subsequent releases were named after dessert or sugary treat in an alphabetical order, with the first few Android versions being called "[Petit Four](/wiki/Petit_Four "Petit Four")", "[Cupcake](/wiki/Cupcake "Cupcake")", "[Donut](/wiki/Donut "Donut")", "[Eclair](/wiki/Eclair "Eclair")",[[38]](#cite_note-hasse27-40) and "[Froyo](/wiki/Frozen_yogurt "Frozen yogurt")", in that order. During its announcement of [Android KitKat](/wiki/Android_KitKat "Android KitKat") in 2013, Google explained that "Since these devices make our lives so sweet, each Android version is named after a dessert", although a Google spokesperson told [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") in an interview that "It's kind of like an internal team thing, and we prefer to be a little bit—how should I say—a bit inscrutable in the matter, I'll say".[[39]](#cite_note-41)

### 2010s

In 2010, Google launched its [Nexus](/wiki/Google_Nexus "Google Nexus") series of devices, a lineup in which Google partnered with different device manufacturers to produce new devices and introduce new Android versions. The series was described as having "played a pivotal role in Android's history by introducing new software iterations and hardware standards across the board", and became known for its "[bloat-free](/wiki/Software_bloat "Software bloat")" software with "timely ... updates".[[40]](#cite_note-42) At its [developer conference](/wiki/Google_I/O "Google I/O") in May 2013, Google announced a special version of the [Samsung Galaxy S4](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S4 "Samsung Galaxy S4"), where, instead of using Samsung's own Android customization, the phone ran "stock Android" and was promised to receive new system updates fast.[[41]](#cite_note-43) The device would become the start of the [Google Play edition](/wiki/List_of_Google_Play_edition_devices "List of Google Play edition devices") program, and was followed by other devices, including the [HTC One](/wiki/HTC_One_(M7) "HTC One (M7)") Google Play edition,[[42]](#cite_note-44) and [Moto G](/wiki/Moto_G_(1st_generation) "Moto G (1st generation)") Google Play edition.[[43]](#cite_note-45) In 2015, *[Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica "Ars Technica")* wrote that "Earlier this week, the last of the Google Play edition Android phones in Google's online storefront were listed as "no longer available for sale" and that "Now they're all gone, and it looks a whole lot like the program has wrapped up".[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/A_Press_Conference_for_the_Launch_of_Nexus_7_on_September_27%2C_2012_in_Seoul_from_acrofan_3.JPG/250px-A_Press_Conference_for_the_Launch_of_Nexus_7_on_September_27%2C_2012_in_Seoul_from_acrofan_3.JPG)](/wiki/File:A_Press_Conference_for_the_Launch_of_Nexus_7_on_September_27,_2012_in_Seoul_from_acrofan_3.JPG)

[Eric Schmidt](/wiki/Eric_Schmidt "Eric Schmidt"), [Andy Rubin](/wiki/Andy_Rubin "Andy Rubin") and [Hugo Barra](/wiki/Hugo_Barra "Hugo Barra") at a 2012 press conference announcing Google's Nexus 7 tablet

From 2008 to 2013, [Hugo Barra](/wiki/Hugo_Barra "Hugo Barra") served as product spokesperson, representing Android at press conferences and [Google I/O](/wiki/Google_I/O "Google I/O"), Google's annual developer-focused conference. He left Google in August 2013 to join Chinese phone maker [Xiaomi](/wiki/Xiaomi "Xiaomi").[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) Less than six months earlier, Google's then-[CEO](/wiki/CEO "CEO") [Larry Page](/wiki/Larry_Page "Larry Page") announced in a blog post that Andy Rubin had moved from the Android division to take on new projects at Google, and that [Sundar Pichai](/wiki/Sundar_Pichai "Sundar Pichai") would become the new Android lead.[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51) Pichai himself would eventually switch positions, becoming the new CEO of Google in August 2015 following the company's restructure into the [Alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.") conglomerate,[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53) making [Hiroshi Lockheimer](/wiki/Hiroshi_Lockheimer "Hiroshi Lockheimer") the new head of Android.[[52]](#cite_note-54)[[53]](#cite_note-55)

On [Android 4.4](/wiki/Android_4.4 "Android 4.4"), *KitKat*, shared writing access to [MicroSD](/wiki/MicroSD "MicroSD") memory cards has been locked for user-installed applications, to which only the dedicated directories with respective package names, located inside `Android/data/`, remained writeable. Writing access has been reinstated with [Android 5](/wiki/Android_5 "Android 5") *Lollipop* through the [backwards-incompatible](/wiki/Backwards_compatibility "Backwards compatibility") *Google Storage Access Framework* [interface](/wiki/API "API").[[54]](#cite_note-56)

In June 2014, Google announced [Android One](/wiki/Android_One "Android One"), a set of "hardware reference models" that would "allow [device makers] to easily create high-quality phones at low costs", designed for consumers in developing countries.[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59) In September, Google announced the first set of Android One phones for release in India.[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61) However, *[Recode](/wiki/Recode "Recode")* reported in June 2015 that the project was "a disappointment", citing "reluctant consumers and manufacturing partners" and "misfires from the search company that has never quite cracked hardware".[[60]](#cite_note-62) Plans to relaunch Android One surfaced in August 2015,[[61]](#cite_note-63) with Africa announced as the next location for the program a week later.[[62]](#cite_note-64)[[63]](#cite_note-65) A report from *The Information* in January 2017 stated that Google is expanding its low-cost Android One program into the United States, although *The Verge* notes that the company will presumably not produce the actual devices itself.[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[65]](#cite_note-67) Google introduced the [Pixel and Pixel XL smartphones](/wiki/Pixel_(smartphone) "Pixel (smartphone)") in October 2016, marketed as being the first phones made by Google,[[66]](#cite_note-68)[[67]](#cite_note-69) and exclusively featured certain software features, such as the [Google Assistant](/wiki/Google_Assistant "Google Assistant"), before wider rollout.[[68]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71) The Pixel phones replaced the Nexus series,[[70]](#cite_note-72) with a new generation of Pixel phones launched in October 2017.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

In May 2019, the operating system became entangled in the [trade war between China and the United States](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war") involving [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei"), which, like many other tech firms, had become dependent on access to the Android platform.[[72]](#cite_note-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75) In the summer of 2019, Huawei announced it would create an alternative operating system to Android[[74]](#cite_note-76) known as [Harmony OS](/wiki/Harmony_OS "Harmony OS"),[[75]](#cite_note-77) and has filed for intellectual property rights across major global markets.[[76]](#cite_note-78)[[77]](#cite_note-79) Under such sanctions Huawei has long-term plans to replace Android in 2022 with the new operating system, as Harmony OS was originally designed for [internet of things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things") devices, rather than for smartphones and tablets.[[78]](#cite_note-verge-harmony-80)

On August 22, 2019, it was announced that Android "Q" would officially be branded as Android 10, ending the historic practice of naming major versions after desserts. Google stated that these names were not "inclusive" to international users (due either to the aforementioned foods not being internationally known, or being difficult to pronounce in some languages).[[79]](#cite_note-81)[[80]](#cite_note-82) On the same day, *Android Police* reported that Google had commissioned a statue of a giant number "10" to be installed in the lobby of the developers' new office.[[81]](#cite_note-83) Android 10 was released on September 3, 2019, to [Google Pixel](/wiki/Google_Pixel "Google Pixel") phones first.

### 2020s

In late 2021, some users reported that they were unable to dial emergency services.[[82]](#cite_note-84)[[83]](#cite_note-85) The problem was caused by a combination of bugs in Android and in the [Microsoft Teams](/wiki/Microsoft_Teams "Microsoft Teams") app; both companies released updates addressing the issue.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

On December 12, 2024, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") announced [Android XR](/wiki/Android_XR "Android XR"). It is a new operating system developed by Google, designed for [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") and [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") devices, such as VR headsets and smart glasses. It was built in collaboration with [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") and [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm"). The platform is also focused on supporting developers with tools like [ARCore](/wiki/ARCore "ARCore") and Unity to build applications for upcoming XR devices.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

In March 2025, Google announced its plans to consolidate Android development to internal sources. While public developers will no longer be able to contribute, the Android source will still be published.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

## Features

Main article: [List of features in Android](/wiki/List_of_features_in_Android "List of features in Android")

### Interface

Android's default user interface is mainly based on [direct manipulation](/wiki/Direct_manipulation_interface "Direct manipulation interface"), using touch inputs that loosely correspond to real-world actions, like swiping, tapping, pinching, and reverse pinching to manipulate on-screen objects, along with a [virtual keyboard](/wiki/Virtual_keyboard "Virtual keyboard").[[87]](#cite_note-89) [Game controllers](/wiki/Game_controller "Game controller") and full-size physical keyboards are supported via [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth") or [USB](/wiki/USB "USB").[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) The response to user input is designed to be immediate and provides a fluid touch interface, often using the vibration capabilities of the device to provide [haptic feedback](/wiki/Haptic_technology "Haptic technology") to the user. Internal hardware, such as [accelerometers](/wiki/Accelerometer "Accelerometer"), [gyroscopes](/wiki/Gyroscope "Gyroscope") and [proximity sensors](/wiki/Proximity_sensor "Proximity sensor") are used by some applications to respond to additional user actions, for example adjusting the screen from portrait to landscape depending on how the device is oriented,[[90]](#cite_note-92) or allowing the user to steer a vehicle in a [racing game](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game") by rotating the device, simulating control of a [steering wheel](/wiki/Steering_wheel "Steering wheel").[[91]](#cite_note-93)

#### Home screen

Android devices boot to the [home screen](/wiki/Home_screen "Home screen"), the primary navigation and information "hub" on Android devices, analogous to the [desktop](/wiki/Desktop_metaphor "Desktop metaphor") found on personal computers. Android home screens are typically made up of app icons and [widgets](/wiki/Software_widget "Software widget"); app icons launch the associated app, whereas widgets display live, auto-updating content, such as a [weather forecast](/wiki/Weather_forecast "Weather forecast"), the user's email inbox, or a [news ticker](/wiki/News_ticker "News ticker") directly on the home screen.[[92]](#cite_note-94) A home screen may be made up of several pages, between which the user can swipe back and forth.[[93]](#cite_note-Design_info-95) Third-party apps available on [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") and other app stores can extensively re-[theme](/wiki/Theme_(computing) "Theme (computing)") the home screen,[[94]](#cite_note-96) and even mimic the look of other operating systems, such as [Windows Phone](/wiki/Windows_Phone "Windows Phone").[[95]](#cite_note-97) Most manufacturers customize the look and features of their Android devices to differentiate themselves from their competitors.[[96]](#cite_note-98)

#### Status bar

Along the top of the screen is a status bar, showing information about the device and its connectivity. This status bar can be pulled (swiped) down to reveal a notification screen where apps display important information or updates, as well as quick access to system controls and toggles such as display brightness, connectivity settings ([WiFi](/wiki/WiFi "WiFi"), Bluetooth, cellular data), audio mode, and [flashlight](/wiki/Flashlight "Flashlight").[[93]](#cite_note-Design_info-95) Vendors may implement extended settings such as the ability to adjust the flashlight brightness.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

#### Notifications

Notifications are "short, timely, and relevant information about your app when it's not in use", and when tapped, users are directed to a screen inside the app relating to the notification.[[98]](#cite_note-100) Beginning with [Android 4.1 "Jelly Bean"](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean "Android Jelly Bean"), "expandable notifications" allow the user to tap an icon on the notification in order for it to expand and display more information and possible app actions right from the notification.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

#### App lists

An "All Apps" screen lists all installed applications, with the ability for users to drag an app from the list onto the home screen. The app list may be accessed using a gesture or a button, depending on the Android version. A "Recents" screen, also known as "Overview", lets users switch between recently used apps.[[93]](#cite_note-Design_info-95)

The recent list may appear side-by-side or overlapping, depending on the Android version and manufacturer.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

#### Navigation buttons

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/HTC_Desire_-_optic_navigation.jpeg/250px-HTC_Desire_-_optic_navigation.jpeg)](/wiki/File:HTC_Desire_-_optic_navigation.jpeg)

Front buttons (home, menu/options, go back, search) and [optical track pad](/wiki/Smartphone#Alternative_input_methods "Smartphone") of an [HTC Desire](/wiki/HTC_Desire "HTC Desire"), a 2010 smartphone with Android OS

Many early Android OS smartphones were equipped with a dedicated search button for quick access to a [web search engine](/wiki/Web_search_engine "Web search engine") and individual apps' internal search feature. More recent devices typically allow the former through a long press or swipe away from the home button.[[101]](#cite_note-MenuKey-103)

The dedicated option key, also known as menu key, and its on-screen simulation, is no longer supported since Android version 10. Google recommends mobile application developers to locate menus within the user interface.[[101]](#cite_note-MenuKey-103) On more recent phones, its place is occupied by a task key used to access the list of recently used apps when actuated. Depending on device, its long press may simulate a menu button press or engage [split screen](/wiki/Split_screen_(computing) "Split screen (computing)") view, the latter of which is the default behaviour since stock Android version 7.[[102]](#cite_note-104)[[103]](#cite_note-105)[[104]](#cite_note-NougatSplit-106)

#### Split-screen view

Native support for split screen view has been added in stock Android version 7.0 *Nougat*.[[104]](#cite_note-NougatSplit-106)

The earliest vendor-customized Android-based smartphones known to have featured a split-screen view mode are the 2012 [Samsung Galaxy S3](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S3 "Samsung Galaxy S3") and [Note 2](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_II "Samsung Galaxy Note II"), the former of which received this feature with the *premium suite* upgrade delivered in [TouchWiz](/wiki/TouchWiz "TouchWiz") with Android 4.1 Jelly Bean.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

#### Charging while powered off

When connecting or disconnecting charging power and when briefly actuating the power button or home button, all while the device is powered off, a visual battery meter whose appearance varies among vendors appears on the screen, allowing the user to quickly assess the charge status of a powered-off device without having to start it. Some display the battery percentage.[[106]](#cite_note-108)

#### Desktop mode

Android has supported a rudimentary Desktop Mode in developer options.[[107]](#cite_note-109) A native desktop mode was rolled out in March 2026 to [Pixel](/wiki/Google_Pixel "Google Pixel") devices from the [Pixel 8](/wiki/Pixel_8 "Pixel 8") onward; tablets received it with [Android 16](/wiki/Android_16 "Android 16") QPR3,[[108]](#cite_note-110) with the intention to roll this feature out to more devices in the future.[[109]](#cite_note-111)

### Applications

See also: [Android software development](/wiki/Android_software_development "Android software development") and [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play")

Most Android devices come with preinstalled Google apps including Gmail, Google Maps, Google Chrome, YouTube, Google Play Movies & TV, and others.

Applications ("[apps](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app")"), which extend the functionality of devices, are written using the [Android software development](/wiki/Android_software_development "Android software development") kit (SDK)[[110]](#cite_note-112) and, often, [Kotlin](/wiki/Kotlin "Kotlin") programming language, which replaced [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") as Google's preferred language for Android app development in May 2019,[[111]](#cite_note-113) and was originally announced in May 2017.[[112]](#cite_note-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115) Java is still supported (originally the only option for user-space programs, and is often mixed with Kotlin), as is [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++").[[114]](#cite_note-116) Java or other JVM languages, such as Kotlin, may be combined with [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)")/C++,[[115]](#cite_note-117) together with a choice of non-default [runtimes](/wiki/Runtime_library "Runtime library") that allow better C++ support.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

The SDK includes a comprehensive set of development tools,[[117]](#cite_note-119) including a [debugger](/wiki/Debugger "Debugger"), [software libraries](/wiki/Software_library "Software library"), a handset [emulator](/wiki/Emulator "Emulator") based on [QEMU](/wiki/QEMU "QEMU"), documentation, sample code, and tutorials. Initially, Google's supported [integrated development environment](/wiki/Integrated_development_environment "Integrated development environment") (IDE) was [Eclipse](/wiki/Eclipse_(software) "Eclipse (software)") using the Android Development Tools (ADT) plugin; in December 2014, Google released [Android Studio](/wiki/Android_Studio "Android Studio"), based on [IntelliJ IDEA](/wiki/IntelliJ_IDEA "IntelliJ IDEA"), as its primary IDE for Android application development. Other development tools are available, including a [native development kit](/wiki/Native_development_kit "Native development kit") (NDK) for applications or extensions in C or C++, [Google App Inventor](/wiki/Google_App_Inventor "Google App Inventor"), a visual environment for novice programmers, and various cross platform mobile web applications frameworks. In January 2014, Google unveiled a framework based on [Apache Cordova](/wiki/Apache_Cordova "Apache Cordova") for porting [Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome") [HTML 5](/wiki/HTML_5 "HTML 5") [web applications](/wiki/Web_app "Web app") to Android, wrapped in a native application shell.[[118]](#cite_note-120) Additionally, [Firebase](/wiki/Firebase "Firebase") was acquired by Google in 2014 that provides helpful tools for app and web developers.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

Android has a growing selection of third-party applications, which can be acquired by users by downloading and installing the application's [APK](/wiki/APK_(file_format) "APK (file format)") (Android application package) file, or by downloading them using an [application store](/wiki/Application_store "Application store") program that allows users to [install, update, and remove applications](/wiki/Package_manager "Package manager") from their devices. [Google Play Store](/wiki/Google_Play_Store "Google Play Store") is the primary application store installed on Android devices that comply with Google's compatibility requirements and license the Google Mobile Services software.[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122)[[121]](#cite_note-geek-poweredby-123) Google Play Store allows users to browse, download and update applications published by Google and third-party developers; as of January 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit), there are more than three million applications available for Android in Play Store.[[122]](#cite_note-3_million_apps-124)[[123]](#cite_note-125) As of July 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit), 50 billion application installations had been performed.[[124]](#cite_note-Google_Play_Hits_1_Million_Apps-126)[[125]](#cite_note-android-compatibility-127) Some carriers offer direct carrier billing for Google Play application purchases, where the cost of the application is added to the user's monthly bill.[[126]](#cite_note-128) As of May 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit), there are over one billion active users a month for Gmail, Android, Chrome, Google Play and Maps.

Due to the open nature of Android, a number of third-party application marketplaces also exist for Android, either to provide a substitute for devices that are not allowed to ship with Google Play Store, provide applications that cannot be offered on Google Play Store due to policy violations, or for other reasons. Examples of these third-party stores have included the [Amazon Appstore](/wiki/Amazon_Appstore "Amazon Appstore"), [GetJar](/wiki/GetJar "GetJar"), and SlideMe. [F-Droid](/wiki/F-Droid "F-Droid"), another alternative marketplace, seeks to only provide applications that are distributed under [free and open source](/wiki/Free_and_open_source "Free and open source") [licenses](/wiki/Free-software_license "Free-software license").[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122)[[127]](#cite_note-wired_alt_app_stores-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130)[[129]](#cite_note-131)

In October 2020, Google removed several Android applications from [Play Store](/wiki/Play_Store "Play Store"), as they were identified breaching its data collection rules. The firm was informed by International Digital Accountability Council (IDAC) that apps for children like *Number Coloring*, *Princess Salon* and *Cats & Cosplay*, with collective downloads of 20 million, were violating Google's policies.[[130]](#cite_note-132)

At the [Windows 11](/wiki/Windows_11 "Windows 11") announcement event in June 2021, [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") showcased the new [Windows Subsystem for Android](/wiki/Windows_Subsystem_for_Android "Windows Subsystem for Android") (WSA) to enable support for the [Android Open Source Project](/wiki/Android_Open_Source_Project "Android Open Source Project") (AOSP), but it has since been deprecated. It was intended to allow users to run [Android apps](/wiki/Android_app "Android app") and games on their Windows 11 desktop.[[131]](#cite_note-133) Microsoft ended WSA support on March 5, 2025.[[132]](#cite_note-134)

### Storage

The storage of Android devices can be expanded using secondary devices such as [SD cards](/wiki/SD_card "SD card"). Android recognizes two types of secondary storage: *portable* storage (which is used by default), and *adoptable* storage. Portable storage is treated as an external storage device. Adoptable storage, introduced on Android 6.0, allows the internal storage of the device to be [spanned](/wiki/Non-RAID_drive_architectures#Concatenation_(SPAN,_BIG) "Non-RAID drive architectures") with the SD card, treating it as an extension of the internal storage. This has the disadvantage of preventing the memory card from being used with another device unless it is [reformatted](/wiki/Disk_formatting "Disk formatting").[[133]](#cite_note-Ars_Technica_review-135)

Android 4.4 introduced the Storage Access Framework (SAF), a set of APIs for accessing files on the device's filesystem.[[134]](#cite_note-anandtech-sdkitkat-136) As of Android 11, Android has required apps to conform to a data privacy policy known as *scoped storage*, under which apps may only automatically have access to certain directories (such as those for pictures, music, and video), and app-specific directories they have created themselves. Apps are required to use the SAF to access any other part of the filesystem.[[135]](#cite_note-:32-137)[[136]](#cite_note-138)[[137]](#cite_note-139)

### Memory management

Since Android devices are usually battery-powered, Android is designed to manage processes to keep power consumption at a minimum. When an application is not in use the system [suspends its operation](/wiki/Process_state "Process state") so that, while available for immediate use rather than closed, it does not use battery power or CPU resources.[[138]](#cite_note-phonedogtask-140)[[139]](#cite_note-141) Android manages the applications stored in memory automatically: when memory is low, the system will begin invisibly and automatically closing inactive processes, starting with those that have been inactive for the longest amount of time.[[140]](#cite_note-phandroidtask-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143) Lifehacker reported in 2011 that third-party task-killer applications were doing more harm than good.[[142]](#cite_note-lifehackertask-144)

### Developer options

Some settings for use by [developers](/wiki/Android_app_development "Android app development") for [debugging](/wiki/Debugging "Debugging") and [power users](/wiki/Power_user "Power user") are located in a "Developer options" sub menu, such as the ability to highlight updating parts of the display, show an overlay with the current status of the touch screen, show touching spots for possible use in [screencasting](/wiki/Screencast "Screencast"), notify the user of unresponsive background processes with the option to end them ("Show all ANRs", i.e. "App's Not Responding"), prevent a Bluetooth audio client from controlling the system volume ("Disable absolute volume"), and adjust the duration of transition animations or deactivate them completely to speed up navigation.[[143]](#cite_note-145)[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147)

Developer options are initially hidden since Android 4.2 "Jelly Bean", but can be enabled by actuating the operating system's build number in the device information seven times. Hiding developers options again requires deleting user data for the "Settings" app, possibly resetting some other preferences, or in recent Android versions, turning off the Developer options master switch.[[146]](#cite_note-148)[[147]](#cite_note-149)[[148]](#cite_note-150)

## Hardware

See also: [Android hardware requirements](/wiki/Android_hardware_requirements "Android hardware requirements")

The main hardware platform for Android is [64-bit](/wiki/64-bit_computing "64-bit computing") [ARM](/wiki/ARM_architecture_family "ARM architecture family") (i.e. the [ARMv8-A](/wiki/ARMv8-A "ARMv8-A") architecture) and the [32-bit](/wiki/32-bit_computing "32-bit computing") [ARMv7-A](/wiki/ARM_architecture_family#32-bit_architecture "ARM architecture family") architecture, with [x86-64](/wiki/X86-64 "X86-64") and the 32-bit [x86](/wiki/X86 "X86") architectures also being officially supported in later versions of Android.[[149]](#cite_note-Android_Lollipop_platform_support-151)[[150]](#cite_note-152)[[151]](#cite_note-153) The unofficial [Android-x86](/wiki/Android-x86 "Android-x86") project provided support for x86 architectures ahead of the official support.[[152]](#cite_note-ARMAN-4.0-on-x86-154)[[153]](#cite_note-auto-155) Since 2012, Android devices with [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") processors began to appear, including phones[[154]](#cite_note-156) and tablets. While gaining support for 64-bit platforms, Android was first made to run on 64-bit x86 and then on [ARM64](/wiki/AArch64 "AArch64"). An unofficial experimental port of the operating system to 64-bit [RISC-V](/wiki/RISC-V "RISC-V") architecture was released in 2021.[[155]](#cite_note-157) 32- and 64-bit MIPS was once supported.

Requirements for the minimum amount of [RAM](/wiki/Random-access_memory "Random-access memory") for smartphones running Android 15 range from 4 GB of RAM for full Android to 2 GB, in which case the smartphone manufacturers must use the [Android Go Edition](/wiki/Android_Go "Android Go").[[156]](#cite_note-158) Android supports all versions of OpenGL ES and [Vulkan](/wiki/Vulkan "Vulkan") (and version 1.1 available for some devices[[157]](#cite_note-159)).

Android devices incorporate many optional hardware components, including still or video cameras, [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System"), [orientation sensors](/wiki/Orientation_sensing "Orientation sensing"), dedicated gaming controls, accelerometers, gyroscopes, barometers, [magnetometers](/wiki/Magnetometer "Magnetometer"), proximity sensors, [pressure sensors](/wiki/Pressure_sensor "Pressure sensor"), thermometers, and [touchscreens](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen"). Some hardware components are not required, but became standard in certain classes of devices, such as smartphones, and additional requirements apply if they are present. Some other hardware was initially required, but those requirements have been relaxed or eliminated altogether. For example, as Android was developed initially as a phone OS, hardware such as microphones were required, while over time the phone function became optional.[[125]](#cite_note-android-compatibility-127) Android used to require an [autofocus](/wiki/Autofocus "Autofocus") camera, which was relaxed to a [fixed-focus](/wiki/Fixed-focus_lens "Fixed-focus lens") camera[[125]](#cite_note-android-compatibility-127) if present at all, since the camera was dropped as a requirement entirely when Android started to be used on [set-top boxes](/wiki/Set-top_box "Set-top box").

In addition to running on smartphones and tablets, several vendors run Android natively on regular PC hardware with a keyboard and mouse.[[158]](#cite_note-160)[[159]](#cite_note-161)[[160]](#cite_note-162)[[161]](#cite_note-163) In addition to their availability on commercially available hardware, similar PC hardware-friendly versions of Android are freely available from the Android-x86 project, including customized Android 4.4.[[162]](#cite_note-164) Using the Android emulator that is part of the [Android SDK](/wiki/Android_SDK "Android SDK"), or third-party emulators, Android can also run non-natively on x86 architectures.[[163]](#cite_note-165)[[164]](#cite_note-166) Chinese companies are building a PC and mobile operating system, based on Android, to "compete directly with Microsoft Windows and Google Android".[[165]](#cite_note-167) The Chinese Academy of Engineering noted that "more than a dozen" companies were customizing Android following a Chinese ban on the use of Windows 8 on government PCs.[[166]](#cite_note-168)[[167]](#cite_note-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170)

## Devices

Android runs on a wide variety of devices such as smartphones, tablets, cars, computers, smart watches, and smart TVs.[[169]](#cite_note-171) However, the vast majority of Android-powered devices are smartphones. Unlike its two main competitors in the mobile operating system space, namely iOS and HarmonyOS, Android devices are made by many different original equipment manufacturers. These OEMs include Samsung, Xiaomi, Vivo, Oppo, iQOO, OnePlus, Honor, Google, Sony, Lenovo, Sharp, Realme, Nothing, and Tecno.[[170]](#cite_note-172)[[171]](#cite_note-173)

## Development

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/AOSP_Android_Stack.png/250px-AOSP_Android_Stack.png)](/wiki/File:AOSP_Android_Stack.png)

The stack of Android Open Source Project

Android is developed by Google until the latest changes and updates are ready to be released, at which point the [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code") is made available to the Android Open Source Project (AOSP),[[172]](#cite_note-174) an open source initiative led by Google.[[173]](#cite_note-175) The first source code release happened as part of the initial release in 2007. All releases are under the [Apache License](/wiki/Apache_License "Apache License").[[174]](#cite_note-176)

The AOSP code can be found with minimal modifications on select devices, mainly the former Nexus and current Android One series of devices.[[175]](#cite_note-177) However, most original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) customize the source code to run on their hardware.[[176]](#cite_note-178)[[177]](#cite_note-179)

Android's source code does not contain the [device drivers](/wiki/Device_driver "Device driver"), often proprietary, that are needed for certain hardware components,[[178]](#cite_note-Building_for_devices-180) and does not contain the source code of [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services"), which many apps depend on. As a result, most Android devices, including Google's own, ship with a combination of [free and open source](/wiki/Free_and_open-source_software "Free and open-source software") and [proprietary](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") software, with the software required for accessing Google services falling into the latter category.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In response to this, there are some projects that build complete operating systems based on AOSP as free software, the first being [CyanogenMod](/wiki/CyanogenMod "CyanogenMod") (see section [Open-source community](#Open-source_community) below).

Google provides annual[[179]](#cite_note-181) Android releases, both for factory installation in new devices, and for [over-the-air](/wiki/Over-the-air_programming "Over-the-air programming") updates to existing devices.[[180]](#cite_note-182) The latest major release is [Android 16](/wiki/Android_16 "Android 16").

The extensive variation of [hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware")[[181]](#cite_note-183) in Android devices has caused significant delays for software upgrades and [security patches](/wiki/Patch_(computing) "Patch (computing)"). Each upgrade has had to be specifically tailored, a time- and resource-consuming process.[[182]](#cite_note-184) Except for devices within the Google Nexus and Pixel brands, updates have often arrived months after the release of the new version, or not at all.[[183]](#cite_note-185) Manufacturers often prioritize their newest devices and leave old ones behind.[[184]](#cite_note-alliance-186) Additional delays can be introduced by wireless carriers who, after receiving updates from manufacturers, further customize Android to their needs and conduct extensive testing on their networks before sending out the upgrade.[[184]](#cite_note-alliance-186)[[185]](#cite_note-187) There are also situations in which upgrades are impossible due to a manufacturer not updating necessary [drivers](/wiki/Device_driver "Device driver").[[186]](#cite_note-188)

The lack of after-sale support from manufacturers and carriers has been widely criticized by consumer groups and the technology media.[[187]](#cite_note-189)[[188]](#cite_note-190)[[189]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_upgrades-191) Some commentators have noted that the industry has a financial incentive not to upgrade their devices, as the lack of updates for existing devices fuels the purchase of newer ones,[[190]](#cite_note-192) an attitude described as "insulting".[[189]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_upgrades-191) *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* complained that the method of distribution for updates is complicated only because manufacturers and carriers have designed it that way.[[189]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_upgrades-191) In 2011, Google partnered with a number of industry players to announce an "Android Update Alliance", pledging to deliver timely updates for every device for 18 months after its release; however, there has not been another official word about that alliance since its announcement.[[184]](#cite_note-alliance-186)[[191]](#cite_note-193)

In 2012, Google began de-coupling certain aspects of the operating system (particularly its central applications) so they could be updated through the [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") store independently of the OS. One of those components, [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services"), is a [closed-source](/wiki/Closed-source "Closed-source") system-level process providing [APIs](/wiki/API "API") for Google services, installed automatically on nearly all devices running [Android 2.2 "Froyo"](/wiki/Android_Froyo "Android Froyo") and higher. With these changes, Google can add new system functions and update apps without having to distribute an upgrade to the operating system itself.[[192]](#cite_note-194) As a result, [Android 4.2 and 4.3 "Jelly Bean"](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean "Android Jelly Bean") contained relatively fewer user-facing changes, focusing more on minor changes and platform improvements.[[193]](#cite_note-195)

[HTC](/wiki/HTC "HTC")'s then-executive Jason Mackenzie called monthly security updates "unrealistic" in 2015, and Google was trying to persuade carriers to exclude security patches from the full testing procedures. In May 2016, [Bloomberg Businessweek](/wiki/Bloomberg_Businessweek "Bloomberg Businessweek") reported that Google was making efforts to keep Android more up-to-date, including accelerated rates of security updates, rolling out technological workarounds, reducing requirements for phone testing, and ranking phone makers in an attempt to "shame" them into better behavior. As stated by *Bloomberg*: "As smartphones get more capable, complex and hackable, having the latest software work closely with the hardware is increasingly important". Hiroshi Lockheimer, the Android lead, admitted that "It's not an ideal situation", further commenting that the lack of updates is "the weakest link on security on Android". Wireless carriers were described in the report as the "most challenging discussions", due to their slow approval time while testing on their networks, despite some carriers, including [Verizon Wireless](/wiki/Verizon_Wireless "Verizon Wireless") and [Sprint Corporation](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation"), already shortening their approval times. In a further effort for persuasion, Google shared a list of top phone makers measured by updated devices with its Android partners, and is considering making the list public.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] Mike Chan, co-founder of phone maker Nextbit and former Android developer, said that "The best way to solve this problem is a massive re-architecture of the operating system", "or Google could invest in training manufacturers and carriers 'to be good Android citizens'".[[194]](#cite_note-196)[[195]](#cite_note-197)[[196]](#cite_note-198)

In May 2017, with the announcement of [Android 8.0](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo"), Google introduced Project Treble, a major re-architect of the Android OS framework designed to make it easier, faster, and less costly for manufacturers to update devices to newer versions of Android. Project Treble separates the vendor implementation (device-specific, lower-level software written by silicon manufacturers) from the Android OS framework via a new "vendor interface". In Android 7.0 and earlier, no formal vendor interface exists, so device makers must update large portions of the Android code to move a device to a newer version of the operating system. With Treble, the new stable vendor interface provides access to the hardware-specific parts of Android, enabling device makers to deliver new Android releases simply by updating the Android OS framework, "without any additional work required from the silicon manufacturers."[[197]](#cite_note-199)

In September 2017, Google's Project Treble team revealed that, as part of their efforts to improve the security lifecycle of Android devices, Google had managed to get the Linux Foundation to agree to extend the support lifecycle of the Linux Long-Term Support (LTS) kernel branch from the 2 years that it has historically lasted to 6 years for future versions of the LTS kernel, starting with Linux kernel 4.4.[[198]](#cite_note-200)

In May 2019, with the announcement of [Android 10](/wiki/Android_10 "Android 10"), Google introduced Project Mainline to simplify and expedite delivery of updates to the Android ecosystem. Project Mainline enables updates to core OS components through the Google Play Store. As a result, important security and performance improvements that previously needed to be part of full OS updates can be downloaded and installed as easily as an app update.[[199]](#cite_note-201)

Google reported rolling out new amendments in Android 12 aimed at making the use of third-party application stores easier. This announcement rectified the concerns reported regarding the development of Android apps, including a fight over an alternative in-app payment system and difficulties faced by businesses moving online because of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19").[[200]](#cite_note-202)

### Linux kernel

Android's [kernel](/wiki/Kernel_(operating_system) "Kernel (operating system)") is based on the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel")'s [long-term support](/wiki/Long-term_support "Long-term support") (LTS) branches. As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit), Android (14) uses versions 6.1 or 5.15 (for "Feature kernels", can be older for "Launch kernels", e.g. android12-5.10, android11-5.4, depending on Android version down to e.g. android11-5.4, android-4.14-stable, android-4.9-q), and older Android versions, use version 5.15 or a number of older kernels.[[201]](#cite_note-203) The actual kernel depends on the individual device.[[202]](#cite_note-204)

Android's variant of the Linux kernel has further architectural changes that are implemented by Google outside the typical Linux kernel development cycle, such as the inclusion of components like device trees, ashmem, ION, and different [out of memory](/wiki/Out_of_memory "Out of memory") (OOM) handling.[[203]](#cite_note-205)[[204]](#cite_note-206) Certain features that Google contributed back to the Linux kernel, notably a power management feature called "wakelocks",[[205]](#cite_note-207) were initially rejected by [mainline kernel](/wiki/Mainline_Linux "Mainline Linux") developers partly because they felt that Google did not show any intent to maintain its own code.[[206]](#cite_note-208)[[207]](#cite_note-hartman-209) Google announced in April 2010 that they would hire two employees to work with the Linux kernel community,[[208]](#cite_note-210) but [Greg Kroah-Hartman](/wiki/Greg_Kroah-Hartman "Greg Kroah-Hartman"), the current Linux kernel maintainer for the stable branch, said in December 2010 that he was concerned that Google was no longer trying to get their code changes included in mainstream Linux.[[207]](#cite_note-hartman-209) Google engineer Patrick Brady once stated in the company's developer conference that "Android is not Linux",[[209]](#cite_note-ars_introduction_developer-211) with *[Computerworld](/wiki/Computerworld "Computerworld")* adding that "Let me make it simple for you, without Linux, there is no Android".[[210]](#cite_note-212) *[Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica "Ars Technica")* wrote that "Although Android is built on top of the Linux kernel, the platform has very little in common with the conventional desktop Linux stack".[[209]](#cite_note-ars_introduction_developer-211)

In August 2011, [Linus Torvalds](/wiki/Linus_Torvalds "Linus Torvalds") said that "eventually Android and Linux would come back to a common kernel, but it will probably not be for four to five years".[[211]](#cite_note-213) In December 2011, Greg Kroah-Hartman announced the start of Android Mainlining Project, which aims to put some Android [drivers](/wiki/Device_driver "Device driver"), patches and features back into the Linux kernel, starting in Linux 3.3.[[212]](#cite_note-214) Linux included the autosleep and wakelocks capabilities in the 3.5 kernel, after many previous attempts at a merger. The interfaces are the same but the upstream Linux implementation allows for two different suspend modes: to memory (the traditional suspend that Android uses), and to disk (hibernate, as it is known on the desktop).[[213]](#cite_note-215) Google maintains a public code repository that contains their experimental work to [re-base](/wiki/Rebasing "Rebasing") Android off the latest stable Linux versions.[[214]](#cite_note-216)[[215]](#cite_note-217)

Android is a [Linux distribution](/wiki/Linux_distribution "Linux distribution") according to the [Linux Foundation](/wiki/Linux_Foundation "Linux Foundation"),[[216]](#cite_note-218) Google's open-source chief [Chris DiBona](/wiki/Chris_DiBona "Chris DiBona"),[[217]](#cite_note-219) and several journalists.[[218]](#cite_note-220)[[219]](#cite_note-221) Others, such as Google engineer Patrick Brady, say that Android is not Linux in the traditional [Unix-like](/wiki/Unix-like "Unix-like") Linux distribution sense; Android does not include the [GNU C Library](/wiki/GNU_C_Library "GNU C Library") (it uses [Bionic](/wiki/Bionic_(software) "Bionic (software)") as an alternative C library) and some other components typically found in Linux distributions.[[220]](#cite_note-APIs-222)

With the release of [Android Oreo](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo") in 2017, Google began to require that devices shipped with new [SoCs](/wiki/System_on_a_chip "System on a chip") had Linux kernel version 4.4 or newer, for security reasons. Existing devices upgraded to Oreo, and new products launched with older SoCs, were exempt from this rule.[[221]](#cite_note-223)[[222]](#cite_note-224)

### Rooting

Main article: [Rooting (Android)](/wiki/Rooting_(Android) "Rooting (Android)")

The [flash storage](/wiki/Flash_storage "Flash storage") on Android devices is split into several partitions, such as `/system/` for the operating system itself, and `/data/` for user data and application installations.[[223]](#cite_note-225)

In contrast to typical [desktop Linux](/wiki/Desktop_Linux "Desktop Linux") distributions, Android device owners are not given [root](/wiki/Superuser "Superuser") access to the operating system and sensitive partitions such as `/system/` are partially [read-only](/wiki/Read-only_memory "Read-only memory"). However, [root access](/wiki/Filesystem_Hierarchy_Standard "Filesystem Hierarchy Standard") can be obtained by exploiting [security flaws](/wiki/Vulnerability_(computing) "Vulnerability (computing)") in Android, which is used frequently by the [open-source community](#Open-source_community) to enhance the capabilities and customizability of their devices, but also by malicious parties to install [viruses](/wiki/Computer_virus "Computer virus") and [malware](/wiki/Malware "Malware").[[224]](#cite_note-226) Root access can also be obtained by [unlocking the bootloader](/wiki/Bootloader_unlocking "Bootloader unlocking") which is available on most Android devices, for example on most [Google Pixel](/wiki/Google_Pixel "Google Pixel"), [OnePlus](/wiki/OnePlus "OnePlus") and [Nothing](/wiki/Nothing_(company) "Nothing (company)") models `OEM Unlocking` option in the developer settings allows the user to unlock the bootloader with [Fastboot](/wiki/Fastboot "Fastboot"), afterward, custom software may be installed. Some OEMs have their own methods. The unlocking process [resets the system to factory state](/wiki/Factory_reset "Factory reset"), erasing all user data.[[225]](#cite_note-227) Proprietary frameworks like [Samsung Knox](/wiki/Samsung_Knox "Samsung Knox") limit or block attempts at rooting. Google's [Play Integrity API](/wiki/Play_Integrity_API "Play Integrity API") allows developers to check for any signs of tampering,[[226]](#cite_note-228) although the fairness of the tests have been criticized.[[227]](#cite_note-229)

### Software stack

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Android-System-Architecture.svg/250px-Android-System-Architecture.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Android-System-Architecture.svg)

Android's architecture diagram [*[obsolete source](/wiki/Wikipedia:AGE_MATTERS "Wikipedia:AGE MATTERS")*]

On top of the Linux kernel, there are the [middleware](/wiki/Middleware "Middleware"), [libraries](/wiki/Software_library "Software library") and [APIs](/wiki/API "API") written in [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)"), and [application software](/wiki/Application_software "Application software") running on an [application framework](/wiki/Application_framework "Application framework") which includes [Java](/wiki/Java_(software_platform) "Java (software platform)")-compatible libraries. Development of the Linux kernel continues independently of Android's other source code projects.

Android uses [Android Runtime](/wiki/Android_Runtime "Android Runtime") (ART) as its runtime environment (introduced in version 4.4), which uses [ahead-of-time (AOT) compilation](/wiki/Ahead-of-time_compilation "Ahead-of-time compilation") to entirely compile the application bytecode into [machine code](/wiki/Machine_code "Machine code") upon the installation of an application. In Android 4.4, ART was an experimental feature and not enabled by default; it became the only runtime option in the next major version of Android, 5.0.[[228]](#cite_note-230) In versions no longer supported, until version 5.0 when ART took over, Android previously used [Dalvik](/wiki/Dalvik_(software) "Dalvik (software)") as a [process virtual machine](/wiki/Process_virtual_machine "Process virtual machine") with [trace-based just-in-time (JIT) compilation](/wiki/Tracing_just-in-time_compilation "Tracing just-in-time compilation") to run Dalvik "dex-code" (Dalvik Executable), which is usually translated from the [Java bytecode](/wiki/Java_bytecode "Java bytecode"). Following the trace-based JIT principle, in addition to [interpreting](/wiki/Interpreter_(computing) "Interpreter (computing)") the majority of application code, Dalvik performs the compilation and [native execution](/wiki/Machine_code "Machine code") of select frequently executed code segments ("traces") each time an application is launched.[[229]](#cite_note-231)[[230]](#cite_note-232)[[231]](#cite_note-brady_dissects_android-233)
For its Java library, the Android platform uses a subset of the now discontinued [Apache Harmony](/wiki/Apache_Harmony "Apache Harmony") project.[[232]](#cite_note-ars-njdk-234) In December 2015, Google announced that the next version of Android would switch to a Java implementation based on the [OpenJDK](/wiki/OpenJDK "OpenJDK") project.[[233]](#cite_note-vb-openjdk-235)

Android's [standard C library](/wiki/C_standard_library "C standard library"), [Bionic](/wiki/Bionic_(software) "Bionic (software)"), was developed by Google specifically for Android, as a derivation of the [BSD](/wiki/Berkeley_Software_Distribution "Berkeley Software Distribution")'s standard C library code. Bionic itself has been designed with several major features specific to the Linux kernel. The main benefits of using Bionic instead of the [GNU C Library](/wiki/GNU_C_Library "GNU C Library") (glibc) or [uClibc](/wiki/UClibc "UClibc") are its smaller runtime footprint, and optimization for low-frequency CPUs. At the same time, Bionic is licensed under the terms of the [BSD licence](/wiki/BSD_licence "BSD licence"), which Google finds more suitable for the Android's overall licensing model.[[231]](#cite_note-brady_dissects_android-233)

Aiming for a different licensing model, toward the end of 2012, Google switched the Bluetooth stack in Android from the GPL-licensed [BlueZ](/wiki/BlueZ "BlueZ") to the Apache-licensed BlueDroid.[[234]](#cite_note-236) A new Bluetooth stack, called Gabeldorsche, was developed to try to fix the bugs in the BlueDroid implementation.[[235]](#cite_note-237)

Android does not have a native [X Window System](/wiki/X_Window_System "X Window System") by default, nor does it support the full set of standard [GNU](/wiki/GNU "GNU") libraries. This made it difficult to port existing Linux applications or libraries to Android,[[220]](#cite_note-APIs-222) until version r5 of the [Android Native Development Kit](/wiki/Android_NDK "Android NDK") brought support for applications written completely in [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)") or [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++").[[236]](#cite_note-238) Libraries written in C may also be used in applications by injection of a small [shim](/wiki/Shim_(computing) "Shim (computing)") and usage of the [JNI](/wiki/Java_Native_Interface "Java Native Interface").[[237]](#cite_note-sdlandroid-239)

In current versions of Android, "[Toybox](/wiki/Toybox "Toybox")", a collection of command-line utilities (mostly for use by apps, as Android does not provide a [command-line interface](/wiki/Command-line_interface "Command-line interface") by default), is used (since the release of Marshmallow) replacing a similar "Toolbox" collection found in previous Android versions.[[238]](#cite_note-toyboxmarsmallow-240)

Android has another operating system, Trusty OS, within it, as a part of "Trusty" "software components supporting a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) on mobile devices." "Trusty and the Trusty API are subject to change. [..] Applications for the Trusty OS can be written in C/C++ (C++ support is limited), and they have access to a small C library. [..] All Trusty applications are single-threaded; multithreading in Trusty userspace currently is unsupported. [..] Third-party application development is not supported in" the current version, and software running on the OS and processor for it, run the "[DRM](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") framework for protected content. [..] There are many other uses for a TEE such as mobile payments, secure banking, full-disk encryption, multi-factor authentication, device reset protection, replay-protected persistent storage, wireless display ("cast") of protected content, secure PIN and fingerprint processing, and even malware detection."[[239]](#cite_note-241)

### Open-source community

See also: [List of custom Android distributions](/wiki/List_of_custom_Android_distributions "List of custom Android distributions")

Android's [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code") is released by Google under an [open-source license](/wiki/Open-source_license "Open-source license"), and its open nature has encouraged a large community of developers and enthusiasts to use the open-source code as a foundation for community-driven projects, which deliver updates to older devices, add new features for advanced users or bring Android to devices originally shipped with other operating systems.[[240]](#cite_note-242) These community-developed releases often bring new features and updates to devices faster than through the official manufacturer/carrier channels, with a comparable level of quality;[[241]](#cite_note-243) provide continued support for older devices that no longer receive official updates; or bring Android to devices that were officially released running other operating systems, such as the [HP TouchPad](/wiki/HP_TouchPad "HP TouchPad"). Community releases often come pre-[rooted](/wiki/Android_rooting "Android rooting") and contain modifications not provided by the original vendor, such as the ability to [overclock](/wiki/Overclock "Overclock") or [over/undervolt](/wiki/Dynamic_voltage_scaling "Dynamic voltage scaling") the device's processor,[[242]](#cite_note-244) or security enhancements beyond what is included in the stock OS.[[243]](#cite_note-:2-245)

[CyanogenMod](/wiki/CyanogenMod "CyanogenMod") was the most widely used community firmware;[[244]](#cite_note-246) after its abrupt discontinuation in 2016, a community [fork](/wiki/Fork_(software_development) "Fork (software development)") known as [LineageOS](/wiki/LineageOS "LineageOS") was established as a spiritual continuation of the project.[[245]](#cite_note-247)

Historically, device manufacturers and mobile carriers have typically been unsupportive of third-party [firmware](/wiki/Firmware "Firmware") development. Manufacturers express concern about improper functioning of devices running unofficial software and the support costs resulting from this.[[246]](#cite_note-htcdev-248) Moreover, modified firmware such as CyanogenMod sometimes offer features, such as [tethering](/wiki/Tethering "Tethering"), for which carriers would otherwise charge a premium. As a result, technical obstacles including locked [bootloaders](/wiki/Bootloader "Bootloader") and restricted access to root permissions are common in many devices.
