However, as community-developed software has grown more popular, and following a statement by the [Librarian of Congress](/wiki/Librarian_of_Congress "Librarian of Congress") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") that permits the "[jailbreaking](/wiki/IOS_jailbreaking "IOS jailbreaking")" of mobile devices,[[247]](#cite_note-249) manufacturers and carriers have softened their position regarding third party development, with some, including [HTC](/wiki/HTC "HTC"),[[246]](#cite_note-htcdev-248) [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola"),[[248]](#cite_note-250) [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung")[[249]](#cite_note-251)[[250]](#cite_note-252) and [Sony](/wiki/Sony_Mobile_Communications "Sony Mobile Communications"),[[251]](#cite_note-253) providing support and encouraging development. As a result of this, over time the need to circumvent hardware restrictions to install unofficial firmware has lessened as an increasing number of devices are shipped with unlocked or unlockable [bootloaders](/wiki/Bootloader "Bootloader"), similar to [Nexus](/wiki/Google_Nexus "Google Nexus") series of phones, although usually requiring that users waive their devices' warranties to do so.[[246]](#cite_note-htcdev-248) However, despite manufacturer acceptance, some carriers in the US still require that phones are locked down.[[252]](#cite_note-254)

Android was also ported by the community to [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.")'s [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone"), [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad") and [iPod touch](/wiki/IPod_touch "IPod touch") devices as a consequence of porting the [Linux kernel on Apple devices](/wiki/Linux_on_Apple_devices "Linux on Apple devices"). In 2010, [OpeniBoot](/wiki/OpeniBoot "OpeniBoot") and subsequently iDroid were released to allow dual booting [iOS](/wiki/IOS_(Apple) "IOS (Apple)") and Android 2.3.3 'Gingerbread' on [jailbroken iPhone or iPod Touch devices](/wiki/IOS_jailbreaking "IOS jailbreaking"),[[253]](#cite_note-255)[[254]](#cite_note-256) which was ultimately declared as discontinued in 2012.[[255]](#cite_note-257) In 2020, [David Wang](/wiki/David_Wang_(hacker) "David Wang (hacker)") under his company Corellium released Project Sandcastle which made Android run on the [iPhone 7](/wiki/IPhone_7 "IPhone 7").[[256]](#cite_note-258)

### Device codenames

Internally, Android identifies each supported device by its **device codename**, a short string,[[257]](#cite_note-259) which may or may not be similar to the model name used in marketing the device. For example, the device codename of the [Pixel smartphone](/wiki/Pixel_(1st_generation) "Pixel (1st generation)") is *sailfish*.

The device codename is usually not visible to the end user, but is important for determining compatibility with modified Android versions. It is sometimes also mentioned in articles discussing a device, because it allows to distinguish different hardware variants of a device, even if the manufacturer offers them under the same name. The device codename is available to running applications under `android.os.Build.DEVICE`.[[258]](#cite_note-260)

## Security and privacy

See also: [Mobile security](/wiki/Mobile_security "Mobile security") and [Comparison of open-source mobile phones](/wiki/Comparison_of_open-source_mobile_phones "Comparison of open-source mobile phones")

In 2020, Google launched the Android Partner Vulnerability Initiative to improve the security of Android.[[259]](#cite_note-261)[[260]](#cite_note-262) They also formed an Android security team.[[261]](#cite_note-263)

### Common security threats

Research from security company [Trend Micro](/wiki/Trend_Micro "Trend Micro") lists premium service abuse as the most common type of Android malware, where text messages are sent from infected phones to [premium-rate telephone numbers](/wiki/Premium-rate_telephone_number "Premium-rate telephone number") without the consent or even knowledge of the user. Other malware displays unwanted and intrusive advertisements on the device, or sends personal information to unauthorised third parties.[[262]](#cite_note-264) Security threats on Android are reportedly growing exponentially; however, Google engineers have argued that the malware and virus threat on Android is being [exaggerated](/wiki/Fear,_uncertainty_and_doubt "Fear, uncertainty and doubt") by security companies for commercial reasons,[[263]](#cite_note-exaggeration-265)[[264]](#cite_note-extremely-266) and have accused the security industry of playing on fears to sell virus protection software to users.[[263]](#cite_note-exaggeration-265) Google maintains that dangerous malware is actually extremely rare,[[264]](#cite_note-extremely-266) and a survey conducted by [F-Secure](/wiki/F-Secure "F-Secure") showed that only 0.5% of Android malware reported had come from the Google Play store.[[265]](#cite_note-267)

In 2021, journalists and researchers reported the discovery of [spyware](/wiki/Spyware "Spyware"), called [Pegasus](/wiki/Pegasus_(spyware) "Pegasus (spyware)"), developed and distributed by a private company which can and has been used to infect both [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") and Android smartphones often – partly via use of [0-day exploits](/wiki/Zero-day_(computing) "Zero-day (computing)") – without the need for any user-interaction or significant clues to the user and then be used to exfiltrate data, track user locations, capture film through its camera, and activate the microphone at any time.[[266]](#cite_note-268) Analysis [of data traffic](/wiki/Packet_analyzer "Packet analyzer") by popular smartphones running variants of Android found substantial by-default data collection and sharing with no opt-out by this [pre-installed software](/wiki/Pre-installed_software "Pre-installed software").[[267]](#cite_note-269)[[268]](#cite_note-270) Both of these issues are not addressed or cannot be addressed by security patches.

#### Scope of surveillance by public institutions

See also: [WARRIOR PRIDE](/wiki/WARRIOR_PRIDE "WARRIOR PRIDE")

As part of the broader [2013 mass surveillance disclosures](/wiki/2013_mass_surveillance_disclosures "2013 mass surveillance disclosures") it was revealed in September 2013 that the American and British intelligence agencies, the [National Security Agency](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency") (NSA) and [Government Communications Headquarters](/wiki/Government_Communications_Headquarters "Government Communications Headquarters") (GCHQ), respectively, have access to the user data on iPhone, BlackBerry, and Android devices. They were reportedly able to read almost all smartphone information, including SMS, location, emails, and notes.[[269]](#cite_note-spiegel20130907-271) In January 2014, further reports revealed the intelligence agencies' capabilities to intercept the personal information transmitted across the Internet by social networks and other popular applications such as *[Angry Birds](/wiki/Angry_Birds "Angry Birds")*, which collect personal information of their users for advertising and other commercial reasons. GCHQ has, according to *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, a [wiki](/wiki/Wiki "Wiki")-style guide of different apps and advertising networks, and the different data that can be siphoned from each.[[270]](#cite_note-angrybirdsatgchq-272) Later that week, the Finnish Angry Birds developer [Rovio](/wiki/Rovio_Entertainment "Rovio Entertainment") announced that it was reconsidering its relationships with its advertising platforms in the light of these revelations, and called upon the wider industry to do the same.[[271]](#cite_note-273)

The documents revealed a further effort by the intelligence agencies to intercept Google Maps searches and queries submitted from Android and other smartphones to collect location information in bulk.[[270]](#cite_note-angrybirdsatgchq-272) The NSA and GCHQ insist their activities comply with all relevant domestic and international laws, although the Guardian stated "the latest disclosures could also add to mounting public concern about how the technology sector collects and uses information, especially for those outside the US, who enjoy fewer privacy protections than Americans."[[270]](#cite_note-angrybirdsatgchq-272)

Leaked documents codenamed [Vault 7](/wiki/Vault_7 "Vault 7") and dated from 2013 to 2016, detail the capabilities of the [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") (CIA) to perform electronic surveillance and [cyber warfare](/wiki/Cyber_warfare "Cyber warfare"), including the ability to compromise the operating systems of most smartphones (including Android).[[272]](#cite_note-274)[[273]](#cite_note-275)

#### Security patches

In August 2015, Google announced that devices in the [Google Nexus](/wiki/Google_Nexus "Google Nexus") series would begin to receive monthly security [patches](/wiki/Patch_(computing) "Patch (computing)"). Google also wrote that "Nexus devices will continue to receive major updates for at least two years and security patches for the longer of three years from initial availability or 18 months from last sale of the device via the [Google Store](/wiki/Google_Store "Google Store")."[[274]](#cite_note-276)[[275]](#cite_note-277)[[276]](#cite_note-278) The following October, researchers at the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") concluded that 87.7% of Android phones in use had known but unpatched [security vulnerabilities](/wiki/Security_vulnerabilities "Security vulnerabilities") due to lack of updates and support.[[277]](#cite_note-279)[[278]](#cite_note-280)[[279]](#cite_note-281) Ron Amadeo of *[Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica "Ars Technica")* wrote also in August 2015 that "Android was originally designed, above all else, to be widely adopted. Google was starting from scratch with zero percent market share, so it was happy to give up control and give everyone a seat at the table in exchange for adoption. [...] Now, though, Android has around 75–80 percent of the worldwide smartphone market—making it not just the world's most popular mobile operating system but arguably the most popular operating system, period. As such, security has become a big issue. Android still uses a software update chain-of-command designed back when the Android ecosystem had zero devices to update, and it just doesn't work".[[280]](#cite_note-282) Following news of Google's monthly schedule, some manufacturers, including Samsung and LG, promised to issue monthly security updates,[[281]](#cite_note-283) but, as noted by Jerry Hildenbrand in *Android Central* in February 2016, "instead we got a few updates on specific versions of a small handful of models. And a bunch of broken promises".[[282]](#cite_note-284)

In a March 2017 post on Google's Security Blog, Android security leads Adrian Ludwig and Mel Miller wrote that "More than 735 million devices from 200+ manufacturers received a platform security update in 2016" and that "Our carrier and hardware partners helped expand deployment of these updates, releasing updates for over half of the top 50 devices worldwide in the last quarter of 2016". They also wrote that "About half of devices in use at the end of 2016 had not received a platform security update in the previous year", stating that their work would continue to focus on streamlining the security updates program for easier deployment by manufacturers.[[283]](#cite_note-285) Furthermore, in a comment to *[TechCrunch](/wiki/TechCrunch "TechCrunch")*, Ludwig stated that the wait time for security updates had been reduced from "six to nine weeks down to just a few days", with 78% of flagship devices in North America being up-to-date on security at the end of 2016.[[284]](#cite_note-286)

Patches to bugs found in the core operating system often do not reach users of older and lower-priced devices.[[285]](#cite_note-287)[[286]](#cite_note-288) However, the open-source nature of Android allows security contractors to take existing devices and adapt them for highly secure uses. For example, Samsung has worked with General Dynamics through their Open Kernel Labs acquisition to rebuild *Jelly Bean* on top of their hardened microvisor for the "Knox" project.[[287]](#cite_note-289)[[288]](#cite_note-290)

### Location-tracking

Android smartphones have the ability to report the location of [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") access points, encountered as phone users move around, to build databases containing the physical locations of hundreds of millions of such access points. These databases form electronic maps to locate smartphones, allowing them to run apps like [Foursquare](/wiki/Foursquare_City_Guide "Foursquare City Guide"), [Google Latitude](/wiki/Google_Latitude "Google Latitude"), [Facebook Places](/wiki/Facebook_Places "Facebook Places"), and to deliver location-based ads.[[289]](#cite_note-291) Third party monitoring software such as TaintDroid,[[290]](#cite_note-292) an academic research-funded project, can, in some cases, detect when personal information is being sent from applications to remote servers.[[291]](#cite_note-293)

### Further notable exploits

In 2018, Norwegian security firm Promon has unearthed a serious Android security hole which can be exploited to steal login credentials, access messages, and track location, which could be found in all versions of Android, including [Android 10](/wiki/Android_10 "Android 10"). The vulnerability came by exploiting a bug in the multitasking system enabling a malicious app to overlay legitimate apps with fake login screens that users are not aware of when handing in security credentials. Users can also be tricked into granting additional permissions to the malicious apps, which later enable them to perform various nefarious activities, including intercepting texts or calls and stealing banking credentials.[[292]](#cite_note-294) *[Avast](/wiki/Avast "Avast") Threat Labs* also discovered that many pre-installed apps on several hundred new Android devices contain dangerous malware and [adware](/wiki/Adware "Adware"). Some of the preinstalled malware can commit ad fraud or even take over its host device.[[293]](#cite_note-295)[[294]](#cite_note-296)

In 2020, the Which? watchdog reported that more than a billion Android devices released in 2012 or earlier, which was 40% of Android devices worldwide, were at risk of being hacked. This conclusion stemmed from the fact that no security updates were issued for the Android versions below 7.0 in 2019. Which? collaborated with the AV Comparatives anti-virus lab to infect five phone models with malware, and it succeeded in each case. Google refused to comment on the watchdog's speculations.[[295]](#cite_note-297)

On August 5, 2020, [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") published a blog urging its users to update their applications to the latest version with regards to a security concern that allowed others to access direct messages. A hacker could easily use the "Android system permissions" to fetch the account credentials in order to do so. The security issue is only with Android 8 ([Android Oreo](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo")) and Android 9 ([Android Pie](/wiki/Android_Pie "Android Pie")). Twitter confirmed that updating the app will restrict such practices.[[296]](#cite_note-298)

### Technical security features

Android applications run in a [sandbox](/wiki/Sandbox_(computer_security) "Sandbox (computer security)"), an isolated area of the system that does not have access to the rest of the system's resources, unless access permissions are explicitly granted by the user when the application is installed, however this may not be possible for pre-installed apps. It is not possible, for example, to turn off the microphone access of the pre-installed camera app without disabling the camera completely. This is valid also in Android versions 7 and 8.[[297]](#cite_note-299)

Since February 2012, Google has used its [Google Bouncer](/wiki/Google_Bouncer "Google Bouncer") malware scanner to watch over and scan apps available in the Google Play store.[[298]](#cite_note-300)[[299]](#cite_note-301) A "Verify Apps" feature was introduced in November 2012, as part of the [Android 4.2 "Jelly Bean"](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean "Android Jelly Bean") operating system version, to scan all apps, both from Google Play and from third-party sources, for malicious behaviour.[[300]](#cite_note-302) Originally only doing so during installation, Verify Apps received an update in 2014 to "constantly" scan apps, and in 2017 the feature was made visible to users through a menu in Settings.[[301]](#cite_note-303)[[302]](#cite_note-304)

In former Android versions, before installing an application, the [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") store displayed a list of the requirements an app needs to function. After reviewing these permissions, the user could choose to accept or refuse them, installing the application only if they accepted.[[303]](#cite_note-305) In [Android 6.0 "Marshmallow"](/wiki/Android_Marshmallow "Android Marshmallow"), the permissions system was changed; apps are no longer automatically granted all of their specified permissions at installation time. An opt-in system is used instead, in which users are prompted to grant or deny individual permissions to an app when they are needed for the first time. Applications remember the grants, which can be revoked by the user at any time. Pre-installed apps, however, are not always part of this approach. In some cases it may not be possible to deny certain permissions to pre-installed apps, nor be possible to disable them. The [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services") app cannot be uninstalled, nor disabled. Any force stop attempt results in the app restarting itself.[[304]](#cite_note-306)[[305]](#cite_note-307) The new permissions model is used only by applications developed for Marshmallow using its [software development kit](/wiki/Software_development_kit "Software development kit") (SDK), and older apps will continue to use the previous all-or-nothing approach. Permissions can still be revoked for those apps, though this might prevent them from working properly, and a warning is displayed to that effect.[[306]](#cite_note-308)[[307]](#cite_note-309)

In September 2014, Jason Nova of *Android Authority* reported on a study by the German security company Fraunhofer AISEC in [antivirus software](/wiki/Antivirus_software "Antivirus software") and malware threats on Android. Nova wrote that "The Android operating system deals with software packages by sandboxing them; this does not allow applications to list the directory contents of other apps to keep the system safe. By not allowing the antivirus to list the directories of other apps after installation, applications that show no inherent suspicious behavior when downloaded are cleared as safe. If then later on parts of the app are activated that turn out to be malicious, the antivirus will have no way to know since it is inside the app and out of the antivirus' jurisdiction". The study by Fraunhofer AISEC, examining antivirus software from [Avast](/wiki/Avast "Avast"), [AVG](/wiki/AVG_AntiVirus "AVG AntiVirus"), [Bitdefender](/wiki/Bitdefender "Bitdefender"), [ESET](/wiki/ESET "ESET"), [F-Secure](/wiki/F-Secure "F-Secure"), [Kaspersky](/wiki/Kaspersky_Lab "Kaspersky Lab"), [Lookout](/w/index.php?title=Lookout_(company)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lookout (company) (page does not exist)"), [McAfee](/wiki/McAfee "McAfee") (formerly Intel Security), [Norton](/wiki/Norton_(software) "Norton (software)"), [Sophos](/wiki/Sophos "Sophos"), and [Trend Micro](/wiki/Trend_Micro "Trend Micro"), revealed that "the tested antivirus apps do not provide protection against customized malware or targeted attacks", and that "the tested antivirus apps were also not able to detect malware which is completely unknown to date but does not make any efforts to hide its malignity".[[308]](#cite_note-310)

In August 2013, Google announced Android Device Manager (renamed Find My Device in May 2017),[[309]](#cite_note-311)[[310]](#cite_note-312) a service that allows users to remotely track, locate, and wipe their Android device,[[311]](#cite_note-313)[[312]](#cite_note-314) with an Android app for the service released in December.[[313]](#cite_note-315)[[314]](#cite_note-316) In December 2016, Google introduced a Trusted Contacts app, letting users request location-tracking of loved ones during emergencies.[[315]](#cite_note-317)[[316]](#cite_note-318) In 2020, Trusted Contacts was shut down and the location-sharing feature rolled into Google Maps.[[317]](#cite_note-319)

On October 8, 2018, Google announced new Google Play store requirements to combat oversharing of potentially sensitive information, including call and text logs. The issue stems from the fact that many apps request permissions to access users' personal information, even if this information is not needed for the app to function, and some users unquestionably grant these permissions. Alternatively, a permission might be listed in the app manifest as required (as opposed to optional) and the app would not install unless user grants the permission; users can withdraw any, even required, permissions from any app in the device settings after app installation, but few users do this. Google promised to work with developers and create exceptions if their apps require phone or SMS permissions for core functionality. The enforcement of this policy began on January 6, 2019, 90 days after its announcement. Furthermore, Google announced a new "target API level requirement" (`targetSdkVersion` in manifest) of at least Android 8.0 (API level 26) for all new apps and app updates. The API level requirement was intended to combat the practice of app developers bypassing some permission screens by specifying early Android versions that had a coarser permission model.[[318]](#cite_note-320)[[319]](#cite_note-321)

#### Verified Boot

The Android Open Source Project implements a [verified boot](/wiki/UEFI#Secure_Boot "UEFI") chain with intentions to [verify](/wiki/Data_verification "Data verification") that executed code, such as the [kernel](/wiki/Kernel_(operating_system) "Kernel (operating system)") or [bootloader](/wiki/Bootloader "Bootloader"), comes from an official source instead of a malicious actor. This implementation establishes a full chain of trust, as it initially starts at a hardware level. Subsequently, the boot loader is verified and system partitions such as `system` and `vendor` are checked for [integrity](/wiki/Data_integrity "Data integrity").[[320]](#cite_note-verifiedboot-322)[[321]](#cite_note-323)

Furthermore, this process verifies that a previous version of Android has not been installed. This effectively provides rollback protection, which mitigates exploits that are similar to a [downgrade attack](/wiki/Downgrade_attack "Downgrade attack").[[320]](#cite_note-verifiedboot-322)

##### dm-verity

See also: [Device mapper](/wiki/Device_mapper "Device mapper")

Android (all supported versions, as far back as version 4.4 of the Android Open Source Project) has the option to provide a [verified boot](/wiki/UEFI#Secure_Boot "UEFI") chain with `dm-verity`. This is a feature in the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel") that allows for transparent integrity checking of [block devices](/wiki/Device_file#Block_devices "Device file").[[322]](#cite_note-324)[[323]](#cite_note-325)

This feature is designed to mitigate persistent [rootkits](/wiki/Rootkits "Rootkits").

### Google Play Services and vendor changes

See also: [Behavioral targeting](/wiki/Behavioral_targeting "Behavioral targeting") and [DeGoogle](/wiki/DeGoogle "DeGoogle")

Dependence on proprietary [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services") and customizations added on top of the operating system by vendors who license Android from Google is causing privacy concerns.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[324]](#cite_note-326)[[325]](#cite_note-327)[[326]](#cite_note-328)

## Criticism and controversy

### Privacy and GDPR compliance

#### France

In 2019, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") was fined €50 million by the French [CNIL](/wiki/Commission_nationale_de_l%27informatique_et_des_libert%C3%A9s "Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés") for a lack of information regarding their users.[[327]](#cite_note-329)

Two years later, in 2021, [researcher](/wiki/Research "Research") Douglas Leith, using a sort of data interception, showed that several data are sent from Android device to [Google](/wiki/Google "Google")'s servers, even when the phone is sleeping (IDLE) with no [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") account registered into it.[[328]](#cite_note-330) Several [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") [applications](/wiki/Application_software "Application software") send data, such as [Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome"), [Message](/wiki/Google_Messages "Google Messages") or [Docs](/wiki/Google_Docs "Google Docs"), however [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") is the only one to add a unique identifier data.[[329]](#cite_note-331)

In 2022, Leith showed that an Android [phone](/wiki/Telephone "Telephone") sent various data related to communications, including [phone](/wiki/Telephone "Telephone") and [text messages](/wiki/Text_messaging "Text messaging") to Google. [Timestamp](/wiki/Timestamping_(computing) "Timestamping (computing)"), sender and receiver, plus several other data, are sent to [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services") infrastructure, even if the "Usage and Diag" feature is disabled. Those data are marked with a Unique Identifier of an Android device, and do not comply with [GDPR](/wiki/General_Data_Protection_Regulation "General Data Protection Regulation").[[330]](#cite_note-332)

#### Australia

In 2022, Google was sanctioned A$60 Million (US$40 million) by the [Federal Court of Australia](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Australia "Federal Court of Australia") for misleading consumers about Google's collection of location data from Android phones. The [Australian Competition and Consumer Commission](/wiki/Australian_Competition_and_Consumer_Commission "Australian Competition and Consumer Commission") (ACCC) found that between January 2017 and December 2018, Google had presented the "Location History" setting as the only Google account setting that affected the collection of location data, whilst another, separate setting that also allowed the collection of location data under "Web & App Activity" was enabled by default. The ACCC estimates that around 1.3 million Australian Google account owners were impacted by this design.[[331]](#cite_note-333) Google has since taken remedial steps to address the behavior.[[332]](#cite_note-334)

#### United States of America

A similar case to the 2019 French case regarding location tracking, was brought in the U.S. in a privacy lawsuit filed by a coalition of attorneys general from 40 U.S. states. A penalty of [USD](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") 391 Million was agreed between Google and the [DoJ](/wiki/Ministry_of_justice "Ministry of justice").[[333]](#cite_note-335) [The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times") released at that time a long-term [investigation](/wiki/Investigative_journalism "Investigative journalism") about those [privacy](/wiki/Privacy "Privacy") concerns.[[334]](#cite_note-336)

### Short software support lifespans

See also: [European Ecodesign Directive § Smartphones, mobile phones, cordless phones and tablets](/wiki/European_Ecodesign_Directive#Smartphones,_mobile_phones,_cordless_phones_and_tablets "European Ecodesign Directive")

Android devices, particularly low-end and mid-range models, have been criticized for their short software support lifespans.[[335]](#cite_note-337) Starting in the 2010s, many users found that their devices received only one or two major updates and a limited number of security patches. This lack of long-term support stemmed from manufacturers' unwillingness to invest in costly software upgrades,[[336]](#cite_note-338) which were often tied to contractual agreements with chipset suppliers like [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm"). As a result, Android developed a reputation for rapid device obsolescence.[[337]](#cite_note-339)[[338]](#cite_note-340)

To address this concern, Google introduced [Project Treble](/wiki/Project_Treble "Project Treble"), a framework designed to streamline the development and deployment of Android updates via [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services"), reducing manufacturers' involvement in the update process.

However, for many devices, significant improvements were still limited by the chipset manufacturers. [Fairphone](/wiki/Fairphone "Fairphone"), a company focused on sustainability, explained that its inability to extend software support was due to Qualcomm's policies rather than its own.[[339]](#cite_note-341) [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") executives also highlighted Android's fragmented update ecosystem in their critiques of the platform, while quietly admitting that Qualcomm had also made it difficult for them to offer updates to the iPhone.[[340]](#cite_note-342)

In response to this problem, several community-driven initiatives emerged to provide alternative operating systems for unsupported devices including [LineageOS](/wiki/LineageOS "LineageOS"), [Sailfish OS](/wiki/Sailfish_OS "Sailfish OS"), [Ubuntu Touch](/wiki/Ubuntu_Touch "Ubuntu Touch"), and [PostmarketOS](/wiki/PostmarketOS "PostmarketOS").[[341]](#cite_note-343)

Starting in 2022, Samsung, the largest Android smartphone manufacturer, announced extended software support from previous two years, first to four years, followed by five years in 2023 and six years in 2024.[[342]](#cite_note-344)

Shortly thereafter, Qualcomm followed suit, offering extending support timelines for OEM building phones with its chipsets, first to seven years in 2024,[[343]](#cite_note-345) followed by eight years in 2025. However, the support commitment was only for its most powerful chipsets, and did not make a similar commitment for chipsets used in low-end and mid-range phones.[[344]](#cite_note-346)

These changes bring Samsung and potentially some Qualcomm-powered devices closer to competing platforms, such as Apple, whose iPhones have received four to eight years of support.[[345]](#cite_note-347)

### The opposite of conventional Linux projects

Android, which runs the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel") is the world's most popular mobile platform. As of September 2024, Android has 71.85% of the global mobile operating system market. Although based on the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel"), Android has very little in common with the conventional desktop Linux software stack predominant in most Linux systems. This is mostly evident in Android not using [GNU](/wiki/GNU "GNU") tools but instead [Toybox](/wiki/Toybox "Toybox") as the [userland](/wiki/Userland_(computing) "Userland (computing)"). Android software run on a [hypervisor](/wiki/Hypervisor "Hypervisor") inside a [Java virtual machine](/wiki/Java_virtual_machine "Java virtual machine"), and therefore is incompatible with most other Linux-based systems.[[346]](#cite_note-348)

However, several observers notices that with years, Android became a really closed-participation project of [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux"),[[347]](#cite_note-349) using it in a completely different way, at the opposite of community-driven solutions.[[348]](#cite_note-350)

## Licensing

The [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code") for Android is [open-source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software"): it is developed in private by Google, with the source code released publicly when a new version of Android is released. Google publishes most of the code (including network and telephony [stacks](/wiki/Solution_stack "Solution stack")) under the [non-copyleft](/wiki/Non-copyleft "Non-copyleft") [Apache License](/wiki/Apache_License "Apache License") version 2.0. which allows modification and redistribution.[[349]](#cite_note-351)[[350]](#cite_note-352) The license does not grant rights to the "Android" trademark, so device manufacturers and wireless carriers have to license it from Google under individual contracts. Associated Linux kernel changes are released under the [copyleft](/wiki/Copyleft "Copyleft") [GNU General Public License](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License") version 2, developed by the [Open Handset Alliance](/wiki/Open_Handset_Alliance "Open Handset Alliance"), with the source code publicly available at all times.[[351]](#cite_note-353) The only Android release which was not immediately made available as source code was the tablet-only 3.0 *Honeycomb* release. The reason, according to [Andy Rubin](/wiki/Andy_Rubin "Andy Rubin") in an official Android blog post, was because *Honeycomb* was rushed for production of the [Motorola Xoom](/wiki/Motorola_Xoom "Motorola Xoom"),[[352]](#cite_note-354) and they did not want third parties creating a "really bad user experience" by attempting to put onto smartphones a version of Android intended for tablets.[[353]](#cite_note-355)

Only the base Android operating system (including some applications) is open-source software, whereas most Android devices ship with a substantial amount of proprietary software, such as [Google Mobile Services](/wiki/Google_Mobile_Services "Google Mobile Services"), which includes applications such as [Google Play Store](/wiki/Google_Play_Store "Google Play Store"), Google Search, and [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services") – a software layer that provides [APIs](/wiki/Application_programming_interface "Application programming interface") for the integration with Google-provided services, among others. These applications must be licensed from Google by device makers, and can only be shipped on devices which meet its compatibility guidelines and other requirements.[[121]](#cite_note-geek-poweredby-123) Custom, certified distributions of Android produced by manufacturers (such as [Samsung Experience](/wiki/Samsung_Experience "Samsung Experience")) may also replace certain stock Android apps with their own proprietary variants and add additional software not included in the stock Android operating system.[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122) With the advent of the [Google Pixel](/wiki/Google_Pixel "Google Pixel") line of devices, Google itself has also made specific Android features timed or permanent exclusives to the Pixel series.[[354]](#cite_note-356)[[355]](#cite_note-357) There may also be "[binary blob](/wiki/Binary_blob "Binary blob")" [drivers](/wiki/Driver_(software) "Driver (software)") required for certain hardware components in the device.[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122)[[178]](#cite_note-Building_for_devices-180) The best known fully open source Android services are the [LineageOS](/wiki/LineageOS "LineageOS") distribution and [MicroG](/wiki/MicroG "MicroG") which acts as an open source replacement of Google Play Services.

[Richard Stallman](/wiki/Richard_Stallman "Richard Stallman") and the [Free Software Foundation](/wiki/Free_Software_Foundation "Free Software Foundation") have been critical of Android and have recommended the usage of alternatives such as [Replicant](/wiki/Replicant_(operating_system) "Replicant (operating system)"), because drivers and firmware vital for the proper functioning of Android devices are usually proprietary, and because the Google Play Store application can forcibly install or uninstall applications and, as a result, invite non-free software. In both cases, the use of closed-source software causes the system to become vulnerable to [backdoors](/wiki/Backdoor_(computing) "Backdoor (computing)").[[356]](#cite_note-stallman2011-358)[[357]](#cite_note-359)

It has been argued that because developers are often required to purchase the Google-branded Android license, this has turned the theoretically open system into a [freemium](/wiki/Freemium "Freemium") service.[[358]](#cite_note-JemielniakPrzegalinska2020-360): 20

### Leverage over manufacturers

Google licenses their Google Mobile Services software, along with the Android trademarks, only to hardware manufacturers for devices that meet Google's compatibility standards specified in the Android Compatibility Program document.[[359]](#cite_note-AndroidCompatibility-361) Thus, forks of Android that make major changes to the operating system itself do not include any of Google's non-free components, stay incompatible with applications that require them, and must ship with an alternative software marketplace in lieu of Google Play Store.[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122) A prominent example of such an Android fork is [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com")'s [Fire OS](/wiki/Fire_OS "Fire OS"), which is used on the [Kindle Fire](/wiki/Kindle_Fire "Kindle Fire") line of tablets, and oriented toward Amazon services.[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122) The shipment of Android devices without GMS is also common in mainland [China](/wiki/China "China"), as Google does not do business there.[[360]](#cite_note-362)[[361]](#cite_note-363)[[362]](#cite_note-364)

In 2014, Google also began to require that all Android devices which license the Google Mobile Services software display a prominent "Powered by Android" logo on their boot screens.[[121]](#cite_note-geek-poweredby-123) Google has also enforced preferential bundling and placement of Google Mobile Services on devices, including mandated bundling of the entire main suite of Google applications, mandatory placement of shortcuts to Google Search and the Play Store app on or near the main home screen page in its default configuration,[[363]](#cite_note-365) and granting a larger share of search revenue to OEMs who agree to not include third-party app stores on their devices.[[364]](#cite_note-366) In March 2018, it was reported that Google had begun to block "uncertified" Android devices from using Google Mobile Services software, and display a warning indicating that "the device manufacturer has preloaded Google apps and services without certification from Google". Users of custom ROMs can register their device ID to their Google account to remove this block.[[365]](#cite_note-367)

Some stock applications and components in AOSP code that were formerly used by earlier versions of Android, such as Search, Music, Calendar, and the location API, were [abandoned](/wiki/Deprecation "Deprecation") by Google in favor of [non-free](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") replacements distributed through Play Store (Google Search, YouTube Music, and Google Calendar) and [Google Play Services](/wiki/Google_Play_Services "Google Play Services"), which are no longer open-source. Moreover, open-source variants of some applications also exclude functions that are present in their non-free versions.[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122)[[366]](#cite_note-368)[[367]](#cite_note-369)[[368]](#cite_note-370) These measures are likely intended to discourage forks and encourage commercial licensing in line with Google requirements, as the majority of the operating system's core functionality is dependent on proprietary components licensed exclusively by Google, and it would take significant development resources to develop an alternative suite of software and APIs to replicate or replace them. Apps that do not use Google components would also be at a functional disadvantage, as they can only use APIs contained within the OS itself. In turn, third-party apps may have dependencies on Google Play Services.[[369]](#cite_note-371)

Members of the Open Handset Alliance, which include the majority of Android OEMs, are also contractually forbidden from producing Android devices based on forks of the OS;[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122)[[370]](#cite_note-Compatibility_FAQ-372) in 2012, [Acer Inc.](/wiki/Acer_Inc. "Acer Inc.") was forced by Google to halt production on a device powered by [Alibaba Group](/wiki/Alibaba_Group "Alibaba Group")'s [Aliyun OS](/wiki/Aliyun_OS "Aliyun OS") with threats of removal from the OHA, as Google deemed the platform to be an incompatible version of Android. Alibaba Group defended the allegations, arguing that the OS was a distinct platform from Android (primarily using [HTML5](/wiki/HTML5 "HTML5") apps), but incorporated portions of Android's platform to allow backwards compatibility with third-party Android software. Indeed, the devices did ship with an application store which offered Android apps; however, the majority of them were [pirated](/wiki/Software_piracy "Software piracy").[[371]](#cite_note-373)[[372]](#cite_note-374)[[373]](#cite_note-375)

## Reception

Android received a lukewarm reaction when it was unveiled in 2007. Although analysts were impressed with the respected technology companies that had partnered with Google to form the Open Handset Alliance, it was unclear whether mobile phone manufacturers would be willing to replace their existing operating systems with Android.[[374]](#cite_note-376) The idea of an open-source, Linux-based [development platform](/wiki/Computing_platform "Computing platform") sparked interest,[[375]](#cite_note-377) but there were additional worries about Android facing strong competition from established players in the smartphone market, such as Nokia and Microsoft, and rival Linux mobile operating systems that were in development.[[376]](#cite_note-378) These established players were skeptical: Nokia was quoted as saying "we don't see this as a threat", and a member of Microsoft's Windows Mobile team stated "I don't understand the impact that they are going to have."[[377]](#cite_note-379)

Since then Android has grown to become the most widely used smartphone operating system[[378]](#cite_note-ars5th-380)[[379]](#cite_note-reisinger2015-381) and "one of the fastest mobile experiences available".[[380]](#cite_note-syrup-382) Reviewers have highlighted the open-source nature of the operating system as one of its defining strengths, allowing companies such as Nokia (Nokia X family),[[381]](#cite_note-383) Amazon (Kindle Fire), [Barnes & Noble](/wiki/Barnes_%26_Noble "Barnes & Noble") ([Nook](/wiki/Barnes_%26_Noble_Nook "Barnes & Noble Nook")), [Ouya](/wiki/Ouya "Ouya"), [Baidu](/wiki/Baidu "Baidu") and others to [fork](/wiki/Fork_(software_development) "Fork (software development)") the software and release hardware running their own customised version of Android. As a result, it has been described by technology website *[Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica "Ars Technica")* as "practically the default operating system for launching new hardware" for companies without their own mobile platforms.[[378]](#cite_note-ars5th-380) This openness and flexibility is also present at the level of the end user: Android allows extensive customisation of devices by their owners and apps are freely available from non-Google app stores and third party websites. These have been cited as among the main advantages of Android phones over others.[[378]](#cite_note-ars5th-380)[[382]](#cite_note-384)

Despite Android's popularity, including an activation rate three times that of iOS, there have been reports that Google has not been able to leverage their other products and web services successfully to turn Android into the money maker that analysts had expected.[[383]](#cite_note-385) *[The Verge](/wiki/The_Verge "The Verge")* suggested that Google is losing control of Android due to the extensive customization and proliferation of non-Google apps and services – Amazon's Kindle Fire line uses [Fire OS](/wiki/Fire_OS "Fire OS"), a heavily modified fork of Android which does not include or support any of Google's proprietary components, and requires that users obtain software from its competing [Amazon Appstore](/wiki/Amazon_Appstore "Amazon Appstore") instead of Play Store.[[120]](#cite_note-ars-irongrip-122) In 2014, in an effort to improve prominence of the Android brand, Google began to require that devices featuring its proprietary components display an Android logo on the boot screen.[[121]](#cite_note-geek-poweredby-123)

Android has suffered from "fragmentation",[[384]](#cite_note-386) a situation where the variety of Android devices, in terms of both hardware variations and differences in the software running on them, makes the task of developing applications that work consistently across the ecosystem harder than rival platforms such as iOS where hardware and software varies less. For example, according to data from [OpenSignal](/wiki/OpenSignal "OpenSignal") in July 2013, there were 11,868 models of Android devices, numerous screen sizes and eight Android OS versions simultaneously in use, while the large majority of iOS users have upgraded to the latest iteration of that OS.[[385]](#cite_note-guardianfrag-387) Critics such as *[Apple Insider](/wiki/Apple_Insider "Apple Insider")* have asserted that fragmentation via hardware and software pushed Android's growth through large volumes of low end, budget-priced devices running older versions of Android. They maintain this forces Android developers to write for the "lowest common denominator" to reach as many users as possible, who have too little incentive to make use of the latest hardware or software features only available on a smaller percentage of devices.[[386]](#cite_note-388) However, OpenSignal, who develops both Android and iOS apps, concluded that although fragmentation can make development trickier, Android's wider global reach also increases the potential reward.[[385]](#cite_note-guardianfrag-387)

### Market share

Main article: [Usage share of operating systems](/wiki/Usage_share_of_operating_systems "Usage share of operating systems")

Android is the largest mobile OS with 76% market share as of Q1 2025.[[387]](#cite_note-389) Android is the most used operating system on phones in many countries, with some countries, such as India, having over 96% market share.[[388]](#cite_note-390) On tablets, usage is more even, as iOS is a bit more popular globally.

Research company Canalys estimated in the second quarter of 2009, that Android had a 2.8% share of worldwide [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") shipments.[[389]](#cite_note-Insider_1-391) By May 2010, Android had a 10% worldwide smartphone market share, overtaking [Windows Mobile](/wiki/Windows_Mobile "Windows Mobile"),[[390]](#cite_note-392) whilst in the US Android held a 28% share, overtaking [iPhone OS](/wiki/IPhone_OS "IPhone OS").[[391]](#cite_note-393) By the fourth quarter of 2010, its worldwide share had grown to 33% of the market becoming the top-selling smartphone platform,[[392]](#cite_note-canalysQ42010-394) overtaking [Symbian](/wiki/Symbian "Symbian").[[393]](#cite_note-395) In the US it became the top-selling platform in April 2011, overtaking [BlackBerry OS](/wiki/BlackBerry_OS "BlackBerry OS") with a 31.2% smartphone share, according to *comScore*.[[394]](#cite_note-396)

By the third quarter of 2011, [Gartner](/wiki/Gartner "Gartner") estimated that more than half (52.5%) of the smartphone sales belonged to Android.[[395]](#cite_note-397) By the third quarter of 2012 Android had a 75% share of the global smartphone market according to the research firm IDC.[[396]](#cite_note-idcQ3-398)

In July 2011, Google said that 550,000 Android devices were being activated every day,[[397]](#cite_note-a550-399) up from 400,000 per day in May,[[398]](#cite_note-400) and more than 100 million devices had been activated[[399]](#cite_note-i/o_2011_stats-401) with 4.4% growth per week.[[397]](#cite_note-a550-399) In September 2012, 500 million devices had been activated with 1.3 million activations per day.[[400]](#cite_note-Google_Team-402)[[401]](#cite_note-Eric_Schmidt-403) In May 2013, at Google I/O, Sundar Pichai announced that 900 million Android devices had been activated.[[402]](#cite_note-404)

Android market share varies by location. In July 2012, "mobile subscribers aged 13+" in the United States using Android were up to 52%,[[403]](#cite_note-405) and rose to 90% in China.[[404]](#cite_note-406) During the third quarter of 2012, Android's worldwide smartphone shipment market share was 75%,[[396]](#cite_note-idcQ3-398) with 750 million devices activated in total. In April 2013, Android had 1.5 million activations per day.[[401]](#cite_note-Eric_Schmidt-403) As of May 2013,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit) 48 billion application ("app") installation have been performed from the Google Play store,[[405]](#cite_note-48bn-407) and by September 2013, one billion Android devices had been activated.[[406]](#cite_note-1bn-408)

As of August 2020,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit) the [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") store had over 3 million Android applications published,[[122]](#cite_note-3_million_apps-124)[[407]](#cite_note-409) and as of May 2016,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit) apps had been downloaded more than 65 billion times.[[408]](#cite_note-410) The operating system's success has made it a target for patent litigation as part of the so-called "[smartphone wars](/wiki/Smartphone_patent_wars "Smartphone patent wars")" between technology companies.[[409]](#cite_note-411)[[410]](#cite_note-412)

Android devices account for more than half of smartphone sales in most markets, including the US, while "only in Japan was Apple on top" (September–November 2013 numbers).[[411]](#cite_note-413) At the end of 2013, over 1.5 billion Android smartphones had been sold in the four years since 2010,[[412]](#cite_note-414)[[413]](#cite_note-415) making Android the most sold phone and tablet OS. Three billion Android smartphones were estimated to be sold by the end of 2014 (including previous years). According to Gartner research company, Android-based devices outsold all contenders, every year since 2012.[[414]](#cite_note-416) In 2013, it outsold Windows 2.8:1 or by 573 million.[[415]](#cite_note-more_than_Windows-417)[[416]](#cite_note-418)[[417]](#cite_note-419) As of 2015,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit) Android has the largest [installed base](/wiki/Installed_base "Installed base") of all operating systems;[[22]](#cite_note-Murky_road_despite_dominance-24) Since 2013, devices running it also sell more than Windows, iOS and Mac OS X devices combined.[[418]](#cite_note-best_selling-420)

According to [StatCounter](/wiki/StatCounter "StatCounter"), which tracks only the use for browsing the web, Android is the most popular mobile operating system since August 2013.[[419]](#cite_note-421) Android is the most popular operating system for web browsing in India and several other countries (e.g. virtually all of Asia, with Japan and North Korea exceptions). According to StatCounter, Android is most used on phones in all African countries, and it stated "mobile usage has already overtaken desktop in several countries including India, South Africa and Saudi Arabia",[[420]](#cite_note-422) with all countries in Africa having done so already in which mobile (including tablets) usage is at 90.46% (Android only, accounts for 75.81% of all use there).[[421]](#cite_note-423)[[422]](#cite_note-424)

While Android phones in the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") almost always include Google's proprietary code (such as Google Play) in the otherwise open-source operating system, Google's proprietary code and trademark is increasingly not used in emerging markets; "The growth of [AOSP](/wiki/Android_Open_Source_Project "Android Open Source Project") Android devices goes way beyond just China [..] ABI Research claims that 65 million devices shipped globally with open-source Android in the second quarter of [2014], up from 54 million in the first quarter"; depending on country, percent of phones estimated to be based only on AOSP source code, forgoing the Android trademark: Thailand (44%), Philippines (38%), Indonesia (31%), India (21%), Malaysia (24%), Mexico (18%), Brazil (9%).[[423]](#cite_note-425)

According to a January 2015 [Gartner](/wiki/Gartner "Gartner") report, "Android surpassed a billion shipments of devices in 2014, and will continue to grow at a double-digit pace in 2015, with a 26 percent increase year over year." This made it the first time that any general-purpose operating system has reached more than one billion end users within a year: by reaching close to 1.16 billion end users in 2014, Android shipped over four times more than [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") and [OS X](/wiki/OS_X "OS X") combined, and over three times more than [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"). Gartner expected the whole mobile phone market to "reach two billion units in 2016", including Android.[[424]](#cite_note-426) Describing the statistics, Farhad Manjoo wrote in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* that "About one of every two computers sold today is running Android. [It] has become Earth's dominant computing platform."[[22]](#cite_note-Murky_road_despite_dominance-24)

According to a [Statistica](/wiki/Statistica "Statistica")'s estimate, Android smartphones had an installed base of 1.8 billion units in 2015, which was 76% of the estimated total number of smartphones worldwide.[[425]](#cite_note-427)[[426]](#cite_note-428)[[c]](#cite_note-430) Android has the largest installed base of any [mobile operating system](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system") and, since 2013, the highest-selling operating system overall[[415]](#cite_note-more_than_Windows-417)[[418]](#cite_note-best_selling-420)[[428]](#cite_note-mahapatra1-431)[[429]](#cite_note-elmer1-432)[[430]](#cite_note-sammobile1-433) with sales in 2012, 2013 and 2014[[431]](#cite_note-434) close to the installed base of all PCs.[[432]](#cite_note-435)

In the second quarter of 2014, Android's share of the global smartphone shipment market was 84.7%, a new record.[[433]](#cite_note-436)[[434]](#cite_note-437) This had grown to 87.5% worldwide market share by the third quarter of 2016,[[435]](#cite_note-438) leaving main competitor [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") with 12.1% market share.[[436]](#cite_note-439)

According to an April 2017 [StatCounter](/wiki/StatCounter "StatCounter") report, Android overtook Microsoft Windows to become the most popular operating system for total Internet usage.[[437]](#cite_note-440)[[438]](#cite_note-441) It has maintained the plurality since then.[[439]](#cite_note-442)

In September 2015, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") announced that Android had 1.4 billion monthly active users.[[440]](#cite_note-443)[[441]](#cite_note-444) This changed to 2 billion monthly active users in May 2017.[[442]](#cite_note-445)[[443]](#cite_note-446)

### Adoption on tablets

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Front_view_of_Nexus_7_%28cropped%29.png/250px-Front_view_of_Nexus_7_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:Front_view_of_Nexus_7_(cropped).png)

The [first-generation Nexus 7](/wiki/Nexus_7_(2012_version) "Nexus 7 (2012 version)") tablet, running Android 4.1 Jelly Bean

Despite its success on smartphones, initially Android tablet adoption was slow,[[444]](#cite_note-447) then later caught up with the iPad, in most countries. One of the main causes was the [chicken or the egg](/wiki/Chicken_or_the_egg "Chicken or the egg") situation where consumers were hesitant to buy an Android tablet due to a lack of high quality tablet applications, but developers were hesitant to spend time and resources developing tablet applications until there was a significant market for them.[[445]](#cite_note-448)[[446]](#cite_note-tabs-449) The content and app "ecosystem" proved more important than hardware [specs](/wiki/Specification_(technical_standard) "Specification (technical standard)") as the selling point for tablets. Due to the lack of Android tablet-specific applications in 2011, early Android tablets had to make do with existing smartphone applications that were ill-suited to larger screen sizes, whereas the dominance of Apple's [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad") was reinforced by the large number of tablet-specific [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") applications.[[446]](#cite_note-tabs-449)[[447]](#cite_note-450)

Despite app support in its infancy, a considerable number of Android tablets, like the [Barnes & Noble Nook](/wiki/Barnes_%26_Noble_Nook "Barnes & Noble Nook") (alongside those using other operating systems, such as the [HP TouchPad](/wiki/HP_TouchPad "HP TouchPad") and [BlackBerry PlayBook](/wiki/BlackBerry_PlayBook "BlackBerry PlayBook")) were rushed out to market in an attempt to capitalize on the success of the iPad.[[446]](#cite_note-tabs-449) *InfoWorld* has suggested that some Android manufacturers initially treated their first tablets as a "Frankenphone business", a short-term low-investment opportunity by placing a smartphone-optimized Android OS (before Android 3.0 *Honeycomb* for tablets was available) on a device while neglecting user interface. This approach, such as with the [Dell Streak](/wiki/Dell_Streak "Dell Streak"), failed to gain market traction with consumers as well as damaging the early reputation of Android tablets.[[448]](#cite_note-451)[[449]](#cite_note-452) Furthermore, several Android tablets such as the [Motorola Xoom](/wiki/Motorola_Xoom "Motorola Xoom") were priced the same or higher than the [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad"), which hurt sales. An exception was the [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com") [Kindle Fire](/wiki/Kindle_Fire "Kindle Fire"), which relied upon lower pricing as well as access to Amazon's ecosystem of applications and content.[[446]](#cite_note-tabs-449)[[450]](#cite_note-453)

This began to change in 2012, with the release of the affordable [Nexus 7](/wiki/Nexus_7_(2012_version) "Nexus 7 (2012 version)") and a push by Google for developers to write better tablet applications.[[451]](#cite_note-454) According to International Data Corporation, shipments of Android-powered tablets surpassed iPads in Q3 2012.[[452]](#cite_note-455)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Nook_Color_Showing_Wikipedia_Index_On_Dolphin_Browser_HD.jpg/250px-Nook_Color_Showing_Wikipedia_Index_On_Dolphin_Browser_HD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nook_Color_Showing_Wikipedia_Index_On_Dolphin_Browser_HD.jpg)

[Barnes & Noble Nook](/wiki/Barnes_%26_Noble_Nook "Barnes & Noble Nook") running Android

As of the end of 2013, over 191.6 million Android tablets had sold in three years since 2011.[[453]](#cite_note-456)[[454]](#cite_note-457) This made Android tablets the most-sold type of tablet in 2013, surpassing iPads in the second quarter of 2013.[[455]](#cite_note-458)

According to StatCounter's web use statistics, as of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit), Android tablets represent the majority of tablet devices used in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") (70%), [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") (65%), while less than half elsewhere, e.g. Europe (44%), Asia (44%), North America (34%) and Oceania/Australia (18%). There are countries on all continents where Android tablets are the majority, for example, Mexico.[[456]](#cite_note-459)

### Platform information

Main article: [Android version history](/wiki/Android_version_history "Android version history")

Android has 72% market share vs Apple's iOS/iPadOS at 28% (on [tablets](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer") alone Android is slightly ahead of Apple's iPadOS globally, with Android at 50.47%, and iPadOS at 49.27%,[[2]](#cite_note-tablet_stats-2) some continents are slightly ahead for either platform, and some are far ahead for either tablet platform, Android tablets area ahead in virtually all countries,[[457]](#cite_note-460) e.g. India, and in Europe, with some notable exceptions such as the US (iPadOS 53%), and in China (iPadOS 64%)). Of all operating systems Android is first at 43%, followed by Windows at 29%, then iOS at 17%. Android 15 is the most popular Android version on smartphones and Android 14 on tablets.

As of August 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Android_(operating_system)&action=edit), Android 15 is most popular Android version on smartphones at 27%,[[458]](#cite_note-461) followed by Android 14 (at 19%, down from 37% peak), 13, 12, 11 and 10. At the end of the year 2025, latest version Android 16 had edged up to send place at 15%.[[459]](#cite_note-462) Android is more used than iOS is virtually all countries, with few exceptions such as iOS has a 58% share in the US. Android 15 is the most-used version on 3 continents, and many countries, including US/North America (38%), Europe, Asia, India, China and Japan.[[460]](#cite_note-463)[[461]](#cite_note-464) Usage of Android 13 and newer, i.e. supported versions, is at 61%, the rest of users are not supported with security updates; with recently supported Android 12 (last security updates in 2025), use is at 73%.

On tablets, Android 14 is the most popular version overall (also in e.g. India, Russia, Australia, Europe and South America), at 20%.[[462]](#cite_note-465)[[463]](#cite_note-466) Usage of Android 13 and newer, i.e. supported versions, is at 41% on Android tablets, and with Android 12, until recently supported, at 51%. The usage share varies a lot by country. 73% of US users use insecure Android tablets, and 78% of UK users, and the very old Android 9.0 Pie is still the most commonly used tablet version in both countries.
