Since April 2024, 85.0% of devices have [Vulkan](/wiki/Vulkan "Vulkan") graphics support (77.6% support Vulkan 1.1 or higher, thereof 6.6% supporting Vulkan 1.3),[[466]](#cite_note-469) the successor to OpenGL. At the same time 100.0% of the devices have support for [OpenGL ES 2.0](/wiki/OpenGL_ES_2.0 "OpenGL ES 2.0") or higher, 95.9% are on [OpenGL ES 3.0](/wiki/OpenGL_ES_3.0 "OpenGL ES 3.0") or higher, and 88.6% are using the latest version OpenGL ES 3.2.

### Application piracy

Paid Android applications in the past were simple to [pirate](/wiki/Copyright_infringement_of_software "Copyright infringement of software").[[467]](#cite_note-piracy-470) In a May 2012 interview with *[Eurogamer](/wiki/Eurogamer "Eurogamer")*, the developers of *[Football Manager](/wiki/Football_Manager "Football Manager")* stated that the ratio of pirated players vs legitimate players was 9:1 for their game *Football Manager Handheld*.[[468]](#cite_note-471) However, not every developer agreed that piracy rates were an issue; for example, in July 2012 the developers of the game *[Wind-up Knight](/wiki/Wind-up_Knight "Wind-up Knight")* said that piracy levels of their game were only 12%, and most of the piracy came from China, where people cannot purchase apps from Google Play.[[469]](#cite_note-472)

In 2010, Google released a tool for validating authorized purchases for use within apps, but developers complained that this was insufficient and trivial to [crack](/wiki/Software_cracking "Software cracking"). Google responded that the tool, especially its initial release, was intended as a sample framework for developers to modify and build upon depending on their needs, not as a finished piracy solution.[[470]](#cite_note-473) Android "Jelly Bean" introduced the ability for paid applications to be encrypted, so that they may work only on the device for which they were purchased.[[471]](#cite_note-474)[[472]](#cite_note-475)

## Legal issues

Further information: [Smartphone patent wars](/wiki/Smartphone_patent_wars "Smartphone patent wars") and [Patent troll](/wiki/Patent_troll "Patent troll")

The success of Android has made it a target for [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent") and [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright") litigation between technology companies, both Android and Android phone manufacturers having been involved in numerous patent lawsuits and other legal challenges.

### Patent lawsuit with Oracle

Main article: [Oracle v. Google](/wiki/Oracle_v._Google "Oracle v. Google")

On August 12, 2010, [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation") sued Google over claimed infringement of copyrights and patents related to the [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") programming language.[[473]](#cite_note-computerworld-476) Oracle originally sought damages up to $6.1 billion,[[474]](#cite_note-477) but this valuation was rejected by a United States federal judge who asked Oracle to revise the estimate.[[475]](#cite_note-478) In response, Google submitted multiple lines of defense, counterclaiming that Android did not infringe on Oracle's patents or copyright, that Oracle's patents were invalid, and several other defenses. They said that Android's Java runtime environment is based on [Apache Harmony](/wiki/Apache_Harmony "Apache Harmony"), a [clean room](/wiki/Clean_room_design "Clean room design") implementation of the Java class libraries, and an independently developed virtual machine called [Dalvik](/wiki/Dalvik_(software) "Dalvik (software)").[[476]](#cite_note-479) In May 2012, the jury in this case found that Google did not infringe on Oracle's patents, and the trial judge ruled that the structure of the Java APIs used by Google was not copyrightable.[[477]](#cite_note-480)[[478]](#cite_note-481) The parties agreed to zero dollars in [statutory damages](/wiki/Statutory_damages "Statutory damages") for a small amount of copied code.[[479]](#cite_note-damages-482) On May 9, 2014, the [Federal Circuit](/wiki/Federal_Circuit "Federal Circuit") partially reversed the district court ruling, ruling in Oracle's favor on the copyrightability issue, and [remanding](/wiki/Remand_(court_procedure) "Remand (court procedure)") the issue of [fair use](/wiki/Fair_use "Fair use") to the district court.[[480]](#cite_note-483)[[481]](#cite_note-cafc_13-1021-484)

In December 2015, Google announced that the next major release of Android ([Android Nougat](/wiki/Android_Nougat "Android Nougat")) would switch to [OpenJDK](/wiki/OpenJDK "OpenJDK"), which is the official open-source implementation of the Java platform, instead of using the now-discontinued Apache Harmony project as its runtime. Code reflecting this change was also posted to the AOSP source repository.[[232]](#cite_note-ars-njdk-234) In its announcement, Google claimed this was part of an effort to create a "common code base" between Java on Android and other platforms.[[233]](#cite_note-vb-openjdk-235) Google later admitted in a court filing that this was part of an effort to address the disputes with Oracle, as its use of OpenJDK code is governed under the [GNU General Public License](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License") (GPL) with a [linking exception](/wiki/GPL_linking_exception "GPL linking exception"), and that "any damages claim associated with the new versions expressly licensed by Oracle under OpenJDK would require a separate analysis of damages from earlier releases".[[232]](#cite_note-ars-njdk-234) In June 2016, a United States federal court ruled in favor of Google, stating that its use of the APIs was fair use.[[482]](#cite_note-485)

In April 2021, the Supreme Court of the United States ruled that Google's use of the Java APIs was within the bounds of fair use, reversing the Federal Circuit Appeals Court ruling and remanding the case for further hearing. The majority opinion began with the assumption that the APIs may be copyrightable, and thus proceeded with a review of the factors that contributed to fair use.[[483]](#cite_note-scotusblog_decision-486)

### Anti-competitive challenges in Europe

Main article: [Antitrust cases against Google by the European Union § Android investigation](/wiki/Antitrust_cases_against_Google_by_the_European_Union#Android_investigation "Antitrust cases against Google by the European Union")

In 2013, [FairSearch](/wiki/FairSearch "FairSearch"), a lobbying organization supported by [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation") and others, filed a complaint regarding Android with the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), alleging that its free-of-charge distribution model constituted anti-competitive [predatory pricing](/wiki/Predatory_pricing "Predatory pricing"). The [Free Software Foundation Europe](/wiki/Free_Software_Foundation_Europe "Free Software Foundation Europe"), whose donors include Google, disputed the Fairsearch allegations.[[484]](#cite_note-fsfe-487) On April 20, 2016, the EU filed a formal [antitrust complaint](/wiki/European_Union_competition_law "European Union competition law") against Google based upon the FairSearch allegations, arguing that its leverage over Android vendors, including the mandatory bundling of the entire suite of proprietary Google software, hindering the ability for competing search providers to be integrated into Android, and barring vendors from producing devices running forks of Android, constituted anti-competitive practices.[[485]](#cite_note-bbc-euandroidantitrust-488) In August 2016, Google was fined US$6.75 million by the Russian [Federal Antimonopoly Service](/wiki/Federal_Antimonopoly_Service "Federal Antimonopoly Service") (FAS) under similar allegations by [Yandex](/wiki/Yandex "Yandex").[[486]](#cite_note-verge-russiafine-489) The European Commission issued its decision on July 18, 2018, determining that Google had conducted three operations related to Android that were in violation of antitrust regulations: bundling Google's search and Chrome as part of Android, blocking phone manufacturers from using forked versions of Android, and establishing deals with phone manufacturers and network providers to exclusively bundle the Google search application on handsets (a practice Google ended by 2014). The EU fined Google for €4.3 billion (about US$5 billion) and required the company to end this conduct within 90 days.[[487]](#cite_note-490) Google filed its appeal of the ruling in October 2018, though will not ask for any interim measures to delay the onset of conduct requirements.[[488]](#cite_note-491)

On October 16, 2018, Google announced that it would change its distribution model for Google Mobile Services in the EU, since part of its revenues streams for Android which came through use of Google Search and Chrome were now prohibited by the EU's ruling. While the core Android system remains free, OEMs in Europe would be required to purchase a paid license to the core suite of Google applications, such as Gmail, Google Maps and the Google Play Store. Google Search will be licensed separately, with an option to include Google Chrome at no additional cost atop Search. European OEMs can bundle third-party alternatives on phones and devices sold to customers, if they so choose. OEMs will no longer be barred from selling any device running incompatible versions of Android in Europe.[[489]](#cite_note-492)

### Others

In addition to lawsuits against Google directly, various [proxy wars](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war") have been waged against Android indirectly by targeting manufacturers of Android devices, with the effect of discouraging manufacturers from adopting the platform by increasing the costs of bringing an Android device to market.[[490]](#cite_note-pcworld-493) Both [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") and Microsoft have sued several manufacturers for patent infringement, with Apple's [legal action against Samsung](/wiki/Apple_Inc._v._Samsung_Electronics_Co.,_Ltd. "Apple Inc. v. Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd.") being a particularly high-profile case. In January 2012, Microsoft said they had signed patent license agreements with eleven Android device manufacturers, whose products account for "70 percent of all Android smartphones" sold in the US[[491]](#cite_note-494) and 55% of the worldwide revenue for Android devices.[[492]](#cite_note-495) These include [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") and [HTC](/wiki/HTC "HTC").[[493]](#cite_note-496) Samsung's patent settlement with Microsoft included an agreement to allocate more resources to developing and marketing phones running Microsoft's Windows Phone operating system.[[490]](#cite_note-pcworld-493) Microsoft has also [tied](/wiki/Tying_(commerce) "Tying (commerce)") its own Android software to patent licenses, requiring the bundling of [Microsoft Office Mobile](/wiki/Microsoft_Office_Mobile "Microsoft Office Mobile") and [Skype](/wiki/Skype "Skype") applications on Android devices to subsidize the licensing fees, while at the same time helping to promote its software lines.[[494]](#cite_note-supersite-androidpatents-497)[[495]](#cite_note-498)

Google has publicly expressed its frustration for the current patent landscape in the United States, accusing Apple, Oracle and Microsoft of trying to take down Android through patent litigation, rather than innovating and competing with better products and services.[[496]](#cite_note-499) In August 2011, Google purchased [Motorola Mobility](/wiki/Motorola_Mobility "Motorola Mobility") for US$12.5 billion, which was viewed in part as a defensive measure to protect Android, since Motorola Mobility held more than 17,000 patents.[[497]](#cite_note-500)[[498]](#cite_note-501) In December 2011, Google bought over a thousand patents from [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM").[[499]](#cite_note-502)

Turkey's competition authority investigations about the default search engine in Android, started in 2017, led to a US$17.4 million fine in September 2018 and a fine of 0.05 percent of Google's revenue per day in November 2019 when Google did not meet the requirements.[[500]](#cite_note-:0-503) In December 2019, Google stopped issuing licenses for new Android phone models sold in Turkey.[[500]](#cite_note-:0-503)

## Other uses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/OUYA-Console-set-h.png/250px-OUYA-Console-set-h.png)](/wiki/File:OUYA-Console-set-h.png)

[Ouya](/wiki/Ouya "Ouya"), a [video game console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") which runs Android

Google has developed several variations of Android for specific use cases, including Android Wear, later renamed [Wear OS](/wiki/Wear_OS "Wear OS"), for wearable devices such as wrist watches,[[501]](#cite_note-504)[[502]](#cite_note-505) [Android TV](/wiki/Android_TV "Android TV") for televisions,[[503]](#cite_note-506)[[504]](#cite_note-507) [Android Things](/wiki/Android_Things "Android Things") for smart or [Internet of things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things") devices and [Android Automotive](/wiki/Android_Automotive "Android Automotive") for cars.[[505]](#cite_note-508)[[506]](#cite_note-509) Additionally, by providing infrastructure that combines dedicated hardware and dedicated applications running on regular Android, Google have opened up the platform for its use in particular usage scenarios, such as the [Android Auto](/wiki/Android_Auto "Android Auto") app for cars,[[507]](#cite_note-510)[[508]](#cite_note-511) and [Daydream](/wiki/Google_Daydream "Google Daydream"), a Virtual Reality platform.[[509]](#cite_note-daydream-512)

The open and customizable nature of Android allows [device makers](/wiki/Original_equipment_manufacturer "Original equipment manufacturer") to use it on other electronics as well, including laptops, [netbooks](/wiki/Netbook "Netbook"),[[510]](#cite_note-513)[[511]](#cite_note-514) and desktop computers,[[512]](#cite_note-desktop_3-515) cameras,[[513]](#cite_note-516) headphones,[[514]](#cite_note-517) [home automation](/wiki/Home_automation "Home automation") systems, game consoles,[[515]](#cite_note-Mirror-518) media players,[[516]](#cite_note-519) satellites,[[517]](#cite_note-520) [routers](/wiki/Router_(computing) "Router (computing)"),[[518]](#cite_note-521) [printers](/wiki/Printer_(computing) "Printer (computing)"),[[519]](#cite_note-522) [payment terminals](/wiki/Payment_terminal "Payment terminal"),[[520]](#cite_note-523) [automated teller machines](/wiki/Automated_teller_machine "Automated teller machine"),[[521]](#cite_note-524) [inflight entertainment systems](/wiki/Inflight_entertainment "Inflight entertainment"),[[522]](#cite_note-525) and [robots](/wiki/Robot "Robot").[[523]](#cite_note-526) Additionally, Android has been installed and run on a variety of less-technical objects, including calculators,[[524]](#cite_note-527) [single-board computers](/wiki/Single-board_computer "Single-board computer"),[[525]](#cite_note-528) [feature phones](/wiki/Feature_phone "Feature phone"),[[526]](#cite_note-529) [electronic dictionaries](/wiki/Electronic_dictionary "Electronic dictionary"),[[527]](#cite_note-530) [alarm clocks](/wiki/Alarm_clock "Alarm clock"),[[528]](#cite_note-531) refrigerators,[[529]](#cite_note-532) [landline](/wiki/Landline "Landline") telephones,[[530]](#cite_note-533) coffee machines,[[531]](#cite_note-534) [bicycles](/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle"),[[532]](#cite_note-535) and mirrors.[[515]](#cite_note-Mirror-518)

[Ouya](/wiki/Ouya "Ouya"), a video game console running Android, became one of the most successful [Kickstarter](/wiki/Kickstarter "Kickstarter") campaigns, [crowdfunding](/wiki/Crowdfunding "Crowdfunding") US$8.5m for its development,[[533]](#cite_note-ouya-536)[[534]](#cite_note-537) and was later followed by other Android-based consoles, such as [Nvidia](/wiki/Nvidia "Nvidia")'s [Shield Portable](/wiki/Shield_Portable "Shield Portable") – an Android device in a [video game controller](/wiki/Video_game_controller "Video game controller") form factor.[[535]](#cite_note-538)

In 2011, Google demonstrated "Android@Home", a home automation technology which uses Android to control a range of household devices including light switches, power sockets and thermostats.[[536]](#cite_note-539) Prototype light bulbs were announced that could be controlled from an Android phone or tablet, but Android head Andy Rubin was cautious to note that "turning a lightbulb on and off is nothing new", pointing to numerous failed home automation services. Google, he said, was thinking more ambitiously and the intention was to use their position as a [cloud](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing") services provider to bring Google products into customers' homes.[[537]](#cite_note-540)[[538]](#cite_note-541)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Android_x86_on_EeePC_701_4G.jpg/250px-Android_x86_on_EeePC_701_4G.jpg)](/wiki/File:Android_x86_on_EeePC_701_4G.jpg)

[Android-x86](/wiki/Android-x86 "Android-x86") running on an ASUS [Eee PC](/wiki/Asus_Eee_PC "Asus Eee PC") netbook

[Parrot](/wiki/Parrot_SA "Parrot SA") unveiled an Android-based [car stereo](/wiki/Automotive_head_unit "Automotive head unit") system known as Asteroid in 2011,[[539]](#cite_note-542) followed by a successor, the touchscreen-based Asteroid Smart, in 2012.[[540]](#cite_note-543) In 2013, [Clarion](/wiki/Clarion_(company) "Clarion (company)") released its own Android-based car stereo, the AX1.[[541]](#cite_note-544) In January 2014, at the [Consumer Electronics Show](/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Show "Consumer Electronics Show") (CES), Google announced the formation of the [Open Automotive Alliance](/wiki/Open_Automotive_Alliance "Open Automotive Alliance"), a group including several major automobile makers ([Audi](/wiki/Audi "Audi"), [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors"), [Hyundai](/wiki/Hyundai_Motor_Company "Hyundai Motor Company"), and [Honda](/wiki/Honda "Honda")) and [Nvidia](/wiki/Nvidia "Nvidia"), which aims to produce Android-based [in-car entertainment](/wiki/In-car_entertainment "In-car entertainment") systems for automobiles, "[bringing] the best of Android into the automobile in a safe and seamless way."[[542]](#cite_note-545)

Android comes preinstalled on a few laptops (a similar functionality of running Android applications is also available in Google's [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS "ChromeOS")) and can also be installed on [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") by end users.[[543]](#cite_note-desktop_2-546)[[544]](#cite_note-desktop_1-547) On those platforms Android provides additional functionality for physical [keyboards](/wiki/Computer_keyboard "Computer keyboard")[[545]](#cite_note-keyboard-548) and [mice](/wiki/Mouse_(computing) "Mouse (computing)"), together with the "[Alt-Tab](/wiki/Alt-Tab "Alt-Tab")" key combination for switching applications quickly with a keyboard. In December 2014, one reviewer commented that Android's notification system is "vastly more complete and robust than in most environments" and that Android is "absolutely usable" as one's primary desktop operating system.[[546]](#cite_note-549)

In October 2015, *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* reported that Android will serve as Google's future main laptop operating system, with the plan to fold ChromeOS into it by 2017.[[547]](#cite_note-chromeconfusion-550)[[548]](#cite_note-samtran-551) Google's Sundar Pichai, who led the development of Android, explained that "mobile as a computing paradigm is eventually going to blend with what we think of as desktop today."[[547]](#cite_note-chromeconfusion-550) Also, back in 2009, Google co-founder Sergey Brin himself said that ChromeOS and Android would "likely converge over time."[[549]](#cite_note-552) Lockheimer, who replaced Pichai as head of Android and ChromeOS, responded to this claim with an official Google blog post stating that "While we've been working on ways to bring together the best of both operating systems, there's no plan to phase out ChromeOS [which has] guaranteed auto-updates for five years".[[550]](#cite_note-Google:_Chrome_OS_here_to_stay-553) That is unlike Android where support is shorter with "[EOL](/wiki/End-of-life_(product) "End-of-life (product)") dates [being..] at least 3 years [into the future] for Android tablets for education".[[551]](#cite_note-554)

At Google I/O in May 2016, Google announced [Daydream](/wiki/Google_Daydream "Google Daydream"), a [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") platform that relied on a smartphone and provided VR capabilities through a [virtual reality headset](/wiki/Virtual_reality_headset "Virtual reality headset") and controller designed by Google itself.[[509]](#cite_note-daydream-512) However, this did not catch on and was discontinued in 2019.[[552]](#cite_note-555)

## Mascot

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Android_robot_%282014-2019%29.svg/120px-Android_robot_%282014-2019%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Android_robot_(2014-2019).svg)

2D Android robot logo (2008–2023)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/2023_Android_Bot.png/120px-2023_Android_Bot.png)](/wiki/File:2023_Android_Bot.png)

3D Android robot logo (2023–present)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Android-robot-googleplex-2008.jpg/120px-Android-robot-googleplex-2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Android-robot-googleplex-2008.jpg)

A giant Android mascot at the [Googleplex](/wiki/Googleplex "Googleplex") in 2008

The mascot of Android is a green [android robot](/wiki/Android_(robot) "Android (robot)"), as related to the software's name. Although it had no official name for a long time, the Android team at Google reportedly call it "Bugdroid".[[553]](#cite_note-556)
In 2024, a Google blog post revealed its official name, "The Bot".[[554]](#cite_note-557)[[555]](#cite_note-558)

It was designed by then-Google graphic designer [Irina Blok](/wiki/Irina_Blok "Irina Blok") on November 5, 2007, when Android was announced. Contrary to reports that she was tasked with a project to create an icon,[[556]](#cite_note-559) Blok confirmed in an interview that she independently developed it and made it [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software"). The robot design was initially not presented to Google, but it quickly became commonplace in the Android development team, with various variations of it created by the developers there who liked the figure, as it was free under a [Creative Commons](/wiki/Creative_Commons "Creative Commons") license.[[557]](#cite_note-560)[[558]](#cite_note-561) Its popularity amongst the development team eventually led to Google adopting it as an official icon as part of the Android logo when it launched to consumers in 2008.
